# RL Batch 1000 V3 Ultra Preview

Third batch with longer chains, more cross-image work, and higher order sensitivity.

## Tool List

- `ImageGeneration`: generation
- `ImageEdit`: editing
- `ImageCrop`: cropping
- `ImageRotate`: rotation
- `ImageFlip`: flipping
- `ImageGrounding`: grounding
- `ImageSAM`: masking
- `ImageExtract`: extraction
- `ImageSR`: super-resolution
- `Overlayer`: overlay
- `OCR`: ocr

## Samples

### rl_batch3_0001 | expert | generate_then_build_dual_panel_comparison_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Generate a sports poster with one athlete and repeated props, isolate the main target object, create an edited variant with forest green stripe pattern, place the original and edited versions into a side-by-side comparison panel, then OCR any major generated headline and include it as a small comparison caption. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: ImageGeneration, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, OCR
- Preferred tool order: ImageGeneration -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> OCR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: The task combines generation, local extraction, controlled object editing, structured comparison layout, and OCR-grounded captioning.
- Constraints: Caption text should come from OCR on the generated image rather than being invented.; The original generated scene should remain intact outside the panel area.; Comparison panel must clearly separate original and edited states.; The edited variant should remain recognizably the same object.
- Success criteria: Hero object is correctly isolated from the generated scene.; Edited variant changes only the intended local attribute.; Comparison panel cleanly presents original versus edited object.; Caption is grounded in visible generated text.
- Failure traps: Guessing a caption rather than OCR-ing visible text.; Losing object identity between original and edited variants.
- Hard negative actions: Placing the panel before both object states exist.; Running OCR before the scene and panel layout stabilize.
- Reward focus: generation reliability, object identity, comparison clarity, evidence-grounded caption
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0002 | expert | fine_grained_multi_instance_storyboard_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: From the busy desk with several similar notebooks and mugs, identify the exact second-from-right striped container, extract it, locally edit its label accent to warm orange, display the edited result in a side panel, and mark the original instance in the cluttered source image with a subtle locator overlay. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Later polish steps must not overwrite earlier localized fixes.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageGrounding, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageGrounding -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: The model must keep track of the same instance across extraction, editing, side-panel display, and source-scene marking.
- Constraints: Source marker must highlight the original object without obscuring it.; Edited side-panel object should remain recognizably linked to the source instance.; The side panel and source scene should coexist clearly in one final layout.; Ordinal instance selection must be exact.
- Success criteria: Correct source instance is targeted.; Edited variant changes only the intended local attribute.; Side panel cleanly displays the edited object.; Original instance is clearly but subtly indicated in the source scene.
- Failure traps: Marking the wrong object in the source after editing the right one.; Selecting the wrong near-duplicate instance.
- Hard negative actions: Skipping the second grounding step for source-marker placement.; Building the final layout before the edited object exists.
- Reward focus: exact instance tracking, edit locality, source-result linkage, final layout clarity
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0003 | expert | layout_rebuild_comparison_translation_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Use the festival one-sheet with multiple text blocks as a structured poster-edit task: localize the title and date blocks, swap them for an indie film retrospective, preserve the remaining layout, re-read the final poster content, and place a short French summary strip at the bottom only after the main layout is stable. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. If a step depends on orientation or localization, resolve that dependency first.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, Overlayer, OCR, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> OCR -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: The model must edit part of the layout, then verify the edited result again and use the verified content to drive a final summary overlay.
- Constraints: Bottom summary strip must remain compact and not cover important footer content.; Final sharpening should not disturb already-corrected text alignment.; Title/date replacement must not disturb secondary layout blocks.; The summary strip should be derived from the final edited poster, not the original one.
- Success criteria: Only target title/date blocks are edited.; Poster hierarchy remains recognizable after local changes.; Final OCR reflects the edited version rather than the original.; Summary strip is grounded in the edited poster content and placed cleanly.
- Failure traps: Covering footer information with the bottom strip.; Generating the summary strip from stale pre-edit OCR.
- Hard negative actions: Using ImageSR before final text additions are done.; Skipping the second OCR pass after edits.
- Reward focus: targeted layout editing, post-edit verification, summary grounding, hierarchy preservation
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0004 | expert | generate_then_build_dual_panel_comparison_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Make a sports poster with one athlete and repeated props; after generation, separate the hero object, edit it locally to add copper stripe pattern, assemble original and edited versions in a comparison strip, then read the main headline and add it as a neat caption line. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Only terminate when each sub-goal has visual evidence of being satisfied.
- Required tools: ImageGeneration, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, OCR
- Preferred tool order: ImageGeneration -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> OCR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: The task combines generation, local extraction, controlled object editing, structured comparison layout, and OCR-grounded captioning.
- Constraints: Caption text should come from OCR on the generated image rather than being invented.; The edited variant should remain recognizably the same object.; Comparison panel must clearly separate original and edited states.; The original generated scene should remain intact outside the panel area.
- Success criteria: Hero object is correctly isolated from the generated scene.; Edited variant changes only the intended local attribute.; Comparison panel cleanly presents original versus edited object.; Caption is grounded in visible generated text.
- Failure traps: Losing object identity between original and edited variants.; Editing the whole scene instead of the hero object.
- Hard negative actions: Placing the panel before both object states exist.; Running OCR before the scene and panel layout stabilize.
- Reward focus: generation reliability, object identity, comparison clarity, evidence-grounded caption
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0005 | expert | fine_grained_multi_instance_storyboard_chain

Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Only terminate when each sub-goal has visual evidence of being satisfied.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageGrounding, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageGrounding -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: The model must keep track of the same instance across extraction, editing, side-panel display, and source-scene marking.
- Constraints: Ordinal instance selection must be exact.; Source marker must highlight the original object without obscuring it.; The side panel and source scene should coexist clearly in one final layout.; Edited side-panel object should remain recognizably linked to the source instance.
- Success criteria: Correct source instance is targeted.; Edited variant changes only the intended local attribute.; Side panel cleanly displays the edited object.; Original instance is clearly but subtly indicated in the source scene.
- Failure traps: Using an overly strong source marker that hides visual evidence.; Marking the wrong object in the source after editing the right one.
- Hard negative actions: Editing before exact instance isolation.; Skipping the second grounding step for source-marker placement.
- Reward focus: exact instance tracking, edit locality, source-result linkage, final layout clarity
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0006 | expert | layout_rebuild_comparison_translation_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Analyze the museum flyer with title, date, and sponsor footer, replace only the main title and date with content about a documentary premiere, keep the rest of the hierarchy stable, then OCR the final poster again and add a compact translated summary strip in Chinese along the bottom before a late sharpen pass. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. If a step depends on orientation or localization, resolve that dependency first.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, Overlayer, OCR, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> OCR -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: The model must edit part of the layout, then verify the edited result again and use the verified content to drive a final summary overlay.
- Constraints: Bottom summary strip must remain compact and not cover important footer content.; Final sharpening should not disturb already-corrected text alignment.; The summary strip should be derived from the final edited poster, not the original one.; Title/date replacement must not disturb secondary layout blocks.
- Success criteria: Only target title/date blocks are edited.; Poster hierarchy remains recognizable after local changes.; Final OCR reflects the edited version rather than the original.; Summary strip is grounded in the edited poster content and placed cleanly.
- Failure traps: Generating the summary strip from stale pre-edit OCR.; Changing the full poster instead of local blocks.
- Hard negative actions: Skipping the second OCR pass after edits.; Using ImageSR before final text additions are done.
- Reward focus: targeted layout editing, post-edit verification, summary grounding, hierarchy preservation
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- Failure traps: Using an overly strong source marker that hides visual evidence.; Marking the wrong object in the source after editing the right one.
- Hard negative actions: Building the final layout before the edited object exists.; Skipping the second grounding step for source-marker placement.
- Reward focus: exact instance tracking, edit locality, source-result linkage, final layout clarity
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- Constraints: Edited side-panel object should remain recognizably linked to the source instance.; Ordinal instance selection must be exact.; The side panel and source scene should coexist clearly in one final layout.; Source marker must highlight the original object without obscuring it.
- Success criteria: Correct source instance is targeted.; Edited variant changes only the intended local attribute.; Side panel cleanly displays the edited object.; Original instance is clearly but subtly indicated in the source scene.
- Failure traps: Using an overly strong source marker that hides visual evidence.; Marking the wrong object in the source after editing the right one.
- Hard negative actions: Building the final layout before the edited object exists.; Skipping the second grounding step for source-marker placement.
- Reward focus: exact instance tracking, edit locality, source-result linkage, final layout clarity
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- Hard negative actions: Skipping the second grounding step for source-marker placement.; Building the final layout before the edited object exists.
- Reward focus: exact instance tracking, edit locality, source-result linkage, final layout clarity

- Failure traps: Losing object identity between original and edited variants.; Editing the whole scene instead of the hero object.

- Why hard: The model must keep track of the same instance across extraction, editing, side-panel display, and source-scene marking.
- Constraints: Ordinal instance selection must be exact.; Edited side-panel object should remain recognizably linked to the source instance.; Source marker must highlight the original object without obscuring it.; The side panel and source scene should coexist clearly in one final layout.
- Success criteria: Correct source instance is targeted.; Edited variant changes only the intended local attribute.; Side panel cleanly displays the edited object.; Original instance is clearly but subtly indicated in the source scene.
- Failure traps: Marking the wrong object in the source after editing the right one.; Using an overly strong source marker that hides visual evidence.
- Hard negative actions: Editing before exact instance isolation.; Building the final layout before the edited object exists.
- Reward focus: exact instance tracking, edit locality, source-result linkage, final layout clarity
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Do not infer hidden content that is not supported by the image.
- Required tools: ImageGeneration, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, OCR
- Preferred tool order: ImageGeneration -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> OCR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: The task combines generation, local extraction, controlled object editing, structured comparison layout, and OCR-grounded captioning.
- Constraints: The edited variant should remain recognizably the same object.; The original generated scene should remain intact outside the panel area.; Caption text should come from OCR on the generated image rather than being invented.; Comparison panel must clearly separate original and edited states.
- Success criteria: Hero object is correctly isolated from the generated scene.; Edited variant changes only the intended local attribute.; Comparison panel cleanly presents original versus edited object.; Caption is grounded in visible generated text.
- Failure traps: Editing the whole scene instead of the hero object.; Guessing a caption rather than OCR-ing visible text.

- Success criteria: Correct source instance is targeted.; Edited variant changes only the intended local attribute.; Side panel cleanly displays the edited object.; Original instance is clearly but subtly indicated in the source scene.
- Failure traps: Selecting the wrong near-duplicate instance.; Marking the wrong object in the source after editing the right one.

Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Later polish steps must not overwrite earlier localized fixes.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, Overlayer, OCR, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> OCR -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: The model must edit part of the layout, then verify the edited result again and use the verified content to drive a final summary overlay.
- Constraints: Title/date replacement must not disturb secondary layout blocks.; Final sharpening should not disturb already-corrected text alignment.; The summary strip should be derived from the final edited poster, not the original one.; Bottom summary strip must remain compact and not cover important footer content.

- Failure traps: Covering footer information with the bottom strip.; Generating the summary strip from stale pre-edit OCR.

- User prompt: For the summit poster with schedule snippet and headline, edit just the title and date to match an indie film retrospective, preserve layout and secondary text, then OCR the edited poster and add a small Chinese summary strip across the bottom before the final clarity step. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, Overlayer, OCR, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> OCR -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: The model must edit part of the layout, then verify the edited result again and use the verified content to drive a final summary overlay.
- Constraints: Title/date replacement must not disturb secondary layout blocks.; Bottom summary strip must remain compact and not cover important footer content.; The summary strip should be derived from the final edited poster, not the original one.; Final sharpening should not disturb already-corrected text alignment.
- Success criteria: Only target title/date blocks are edited.; Poster hierarchy remains recognizable after local changes.; Final OCR reflects the edited version rather than the original.; Summary strip is grounded in the edited poster content and placed cleanly.
- Failure traps: Generating the summary strip from stale pre-edit OCR.; Covering footer information with the bottom strip.
- Hard negative actions: Skipping the second OCR pass after edits.; Using ImageSR before final text additions are done.
- Reward focus: targeted layout editing, post-edit verification, summary grounding, hierarchy preservation
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0358 | expert | generate_then_build_dual_panel_comparison_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Generate a travel card layout with a hero sign and secondary icons, isolate the main target object, create an edited variant with deep blue trim, place the original and edited versions into a side-by-side comparison panel, then OCR any major generated headline and include it as a small comparison caption. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Only terminate when each sub-goal has visual evidence of being satisfied.
- Required tools: ImageGeneration, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, OCR
- Preferred tool order: ImageGeneration -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> OCR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: The task combines generation, local extraction, controlled object editing, structured comparison layout, and OCR-grounded captioning.
- Constraints: Comparison panel must clearly separate original and edited states.; The edited variant should remain recognizably the same object.; The original generated scene should remain intact outside the panel area.; Caption text should come from OCR on the generated image rather than being invented.
- Success criteria: Hero object is correctly isolated from the generated scene.; Edited variant changes only the intended local attribute.; Comparison panel cleanly presents original versus edited object.; Caption is grounded in visible generated text.
- Failure traps: Editing the whole scene instead of the hero object.; Losing object identity between original and edited variants.
- Hard negative actions: Running OCR before the scene and panel layout stabilize.; Placing the panel before both object states exist.
- Reward focus: generation reliability, object identity, comparison clarity, evidence-grounded caption
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0359 | expert | fine_grained_multi_instance_storyboard_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Use the crowded market display with repeated fruit labels to run a two-view task: isolate the front-most yellow bottle, create an edited version with matte cream surface finish, show that edited version separately, and add a subtle source marker on the original instance inside the scene. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Only terminate when each sub-goal has visual evidence of being satisfied.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageGrounding, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageGrounding -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: The model must keep track of the same instance across extraction, editing, side-panel display, and source-scene marking.
- Constraints: Source marker must highlight the original object without obscuring it.; Edited side-panel object should remain recognizably linked to the source instance.; The side panel and source scene should coexist clearly in one final layout.; Ordinal instance selection must be exact.
- Success criteria: Correct source instance is targeted.; Edited variant changes only the intended local attribute.; Side panel cleanly displays the edited object.; Original instance is clearly but subtly indicated in the source scene.
- Failure traps: Using an overly strong source marker that hides visual evidence.; Marking the wrong object in the source after editing the right one.
- Hard negative actions: Building the final layout before the edited object exists.; Skipping the second grounding step for source-marker placement.
- Reward focus: exact instance tracking, edit locality, source-result linkage, final layout clarity
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0501 | extreme | recover_replace_curve_reinsert_verify_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: For the curved cosmetic bottle label, recover distorted text through minimal geometric correction, crop and enhance the local text area, read it, extract the actual surface patch, replace the wording with 'LIMITED BLEND', and place the edited patch back without flattening the surface. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Later polish steps must not overwrite earlier localized fixes.
- Required tools: ImageRotate, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageRotate -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is not just text replacement; it is a recover-extract-edit-reinsert cycle where each step preserves geometry for the next.
- Constraints: Replacement wording must inherit the original curvature and local texture logic.; Recovery steps should not permanently flatten the final surface.; Extraction must correspond to the true text-bearing patch, not a surrounding decorative area.; Reinsertion should preserve edge continuity around the patch.
- Success criteria: Original wording becomes readable enough to guide replacement.; Correct local patch is extracted and edited.; Final text follows curved geometry naturally.; Patch boundaries are visually integrated after reinsertion.
- Failure traps: Extracting too large a patch and damaging surrounding graphics.; Returning a flat-looking text block to a curved surface.
- Hard negative actions: Treating the rotated recovery view as the final output image.; Using ImageEdit before text recovery and patch isolation.
- Reward focus: minimal correction, text recovery, patch extraction, surface reintegration
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0502 | extreme | document_reverse_recover_translate_relayout_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Take the reversed menu board taken through glass, correct the mirrored and angled reading direction, isolate the important text region, enhance it, recover the text, translate the main message into Japanese, place the translated version as a clean summary block beside the original, and only then sharpen the final result. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. If a step depends on orientation or localization, resolve that dependency first.
- Required tools: ImageFlip, ImageRotate, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: ImageFlip -> ImageRotate -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This is a long dependency chain where geometry, readability, translation relayout, and late enhancement all interact.
- Constraints: Main message translation should summarize the source, not rewrite unrelated text.; Translation panel should not occlude the original source evidence.; Reading direction fixes must precede OCR.; The last sharpening pass must not damage already-corrected text edges.
- Success criteria: Reversal and tilt are corrected before OCR.; Main text becomes readable enough to transcribe.; Translated summary panel is cleanly placed beside the source.; Final image remains legible in both original and translated areas.
- Failure traps: Placing the translation over the evidence region.; Over-sharpening before OCR and making characters noisy.
- Hard negative actions: Applying the final ImageSR before Overlayer.; Using OCR on the raw image.

### rl_batch3_0503 | extreme | weather_text_restore_dual_surface_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Turn the street cafe terrace with menu board and window sticker into light rain at dusk, but preserve two different text-bearing surfaces: first recover what those surfaces say, then apply the weather edit, repair or reinsert any damaged text areas locally, and sharpen only after both surfaces are stable. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Do not infer hidden content that is not supported by the image.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This couples one global edit with two separate local text-preservation obligations, forcing the agent to track multiple fragile regions through the chain.
- Constraints: Global weather change should not be reverted when doing local text repair.; Both text-bearing surfaces must be considered, not just the most prominent one.; Text restoration must remain confined to damaged surface regions.; Final sharpening should not create halos around repaired text edges.
- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Undoing the weather effect around repaired text regions.; Sharpening restored surfaces until they look pasted on.
- Hard negative actions: Running ImageSR before local text restoration is finished.; Repairing text globally instead of surface-locally.
- Reward focus: global-local coordination, multi-surface preservation, attachment realism, late enhancement discipline
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. If a step depends on orientation or localization, resolve that dependency first.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This couples one global edit with two separate local text-preservation obligations, forcing the agent to track multiple fragile regions through the chain.
- Constraints: Global weather change should not be reverted when doing local text repair.; Both text-bearing surfaces must be considered, not just the most prominent one.; Text restoration must remain confined to damaged surface regions.; Final sharpening should not create halos around repaired text edges.
- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Saving only the main sign while forgetting the secondary printed surface.; Sharpening restored surfaces until they look pasted on.
- Hard negative actions: Repairing text globally instead of surface-locally.; Applying the weather edit before recovering what the text says.
- Reward focus: global-local coordination, multi-surface preservation, attachment realism, late enhancement discipline
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0510 | extreme | recover_replace_curve_reinsert_verify_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Use the helmet sticker photo with curved lettering to run a full recover-and-reinsert cycle: make the text readable, extract the correct curved patch, rewrite it to 'ROUTE 24', and restore it to the same surface with believable material continuity. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Only terminate when each sub-goal has visual evidence of being satisfied.
- Required tools: ImageRotate, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageRotate -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is not just text replacement; it is a recover-extract-edit-reinsert cycle where each step preserves geometry for the next.
- Constraints: Extraction must correspond to the true text-bearing patch, not a surrounding decorative area.; Replacement wording must inherit the original curvature and local texture logic.; Recovery steps should not permanently flatten the final surface.; Reinsertion should preserve edge continuity around the patch.
- Success criteria: Original wording becomes readable enough to guide replacement.; Correct local patch is extracted and edited.; Final text follows curved geometry naturally.; Patch boundaries are visually integrated after reinsertion.
- Failure traps: Returning a flat-looking text block to a curved surface.; Editing text directly on the raw warped region without recovery.
- Hard negative actions: Using ImageEdit before text recovery and patch isolation.; Skipping extraction and trying to overlay flat text back onto the surface.
- Reward focus: minimal correction, text recovery, patch extraction, surface reintegration
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0511 | extreme | document_reverse_recover_translate_relayout_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Recover the crooked bilingual instruction card in a selfie shot into readable form, extract the main message, translate it into Spanish, relayout the translation in a clean side block next to the source document, and apply only late-stage enhancement. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Do not infer hidden content that is not supported by the image.
- Required tools: ImageFlip, ImageRotate, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: ImageFlip -> ImageRotate -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This is a long dependency chain where geometry, readability, translation relayout, and late enhancement all interact.
- Constraints: Reading direction fixes must precede OCR.; The last sharpening pass must not damage already-corrected text edges.; Main message translation should summarize the source, not rewrite unrelated text.; Translation panel should not occlude the original source evidence.
- Success criteria: Reversal and tilt are corrected before OCR.; Main text becomes readable enough to transcribe.; Translated summary panel is cleanly placed beside the source.; Final image remains legible in both original and translated areas.
- Failure traps: Placing the translation over the evidence region.; Over-sharpening before OCR and making characters noisy.
- Hard negative actions: Using OCR on the raw image.; Editing the whole image globally instead of isolating a translation panel.
- Reward focus: orientation dependency, text recovery, translation block layout, late-stage preservation
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.

- Constraints: Replacement wording must inherit the original curvature and local texture logic.; Recovery steps should not permanently flatten the final surface.; Reinsertion should preserve edge continuity around the patch.; Extraction must correspond to the true text-bearing patch, not a surrounding decorative area.
- Success criteria: Original wording becomes readable enough to guide replacement.; Correct local patch is extracted and edited.; Final text follows curved geometry naturally.; Patch boundaries are visually integrated after reinsertion.
- Failure traps: Editing text directly on the raw warped region without recovery.; Returning a flat-looking text block to a curved surface.
- Hard negative actions: Treating the rotated recovery view as the final output image.; Using ImageEdit before text recovery and patch isolation.
- Reward focus: minimal correction, text recovery, patch extraction, surface reintegration
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0527 | extreme | weather_text_restore_dual_surface_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Make the market stall with hanging sign and package labels feel like overcast drizzle, but protect two separate readable surfaces. Recover their wording first, edit the scene globally, then locally repair or reapply damaged text areas and only then enhance final clarity. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This couples one global edit with two separate local text-preservation obligations, forcing the agent to track multiple fragile regions through the chain.
- Constraints: Text restoration must remain confined to damaged surface regions.; Final sharpening should not create halos around repaired text edges.; Global weather change should not be reverted when doing local text repair.; Both text-bearing surfaces must be considered, not just the most prominent one.
- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Undoing the weather effect around repaired text regions.; Sharpening restored surfaces until they look pasted on.
- Hard negative actions: Repairing text globally instead of surface-locally.; Applying the weather edit before recovering what the text says.
- Reward focus: global-local coordination, multi-surface preservation, attachment realism, late enhancement discipline
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- User prompt: For the bus stop ad scene with printed poster and bench label, change the atmosphere to fresh snowfall while keeping both the main sign and the secondary printed surface readable; OCR them first, perform the global weather edit, then locally restore whichever text-bearing surfaces need repair before a final clarity pass. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Only terminate when each sub-goal has visual evidence of being satisfied.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This couples one global edit with two separate local text-preservation obligations, forcing the agent to track multiple fragile regions through the chain.
- Constraints: Global weather change should not be reverted when doing local text repair.; Final sharpening should not create halos around repaired text edges.; Text restoration must remain confined to damaged surface regions.; Both text-bearing surfaces must be considered, not just the most prominent one.
- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Sharpening restored surfaces until they look pasted on.; Undoing the weather effect around repaired text regions.
- Hard negative actions: Applying the weather edit before recovering what the text says.; Running ImageSR before local text restoration is finished.
- Reward focus: global-local coordination, multi-surface preservation, attachment realism, late enhancement discipline
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0543 | extreme | recover_replace_curve_reinsert_verify_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Use the paper bowl rim graphic with bent wording to run a full recover-and-reinsert cycle: make the text readable, extract the correct curved patch, rewrite it to 'NIGHT RIDE', and restore it to the same surface with believable material continuity. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: ImageRotate, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageRotate -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is not just text replacement; it is a recover-extract-edit-reinsert cycle where each step preserves geometry for the next.
- Constraints: Replacement wording must inherit the original curvature and local texture logic.; Recovery steps should not permanently flatten the final surface.; Extraction must correspond to the true text-bearing patch, not a surrounding decorative area.; Reinsertion should preserve edge continuity around the patch.
- Success criteria: Original wording becomes readable enough to guide replacement.; Correct local patch is extracted and edited.; Final text follows curved geometry naturally.; Patch boundaries are visually integrated after reinsertion.
- Failure traps: Editing text directly on the raw warped region without recovery.; Returning a flat-looking text block to a curved surface.
- Hard negative actions: Using ImageEdit before text recovery and patch isolation.; Skipping extraction and trying to overlay flat text back onto the surface.
- Reward focus: minimal correction, text recovery, patch extraction, surface reintegration
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0544 | extreme | document_reverse_recover_translate_relayout_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Recover the crooked bilingual instruction card in a selfie shot into readable form, extract the main message, translate it into Spanish, relayout the translation in a clean side block next to the source document, and apply only late-stage enhancement. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Do not infer hidden content that is not supported by the image.
- Required tools: ImageFlip, ImageRotate, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: ImageFlip -> ImageRotate -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This is a long dependency chain where geometry, readability, translation relayout, and late enhancement all interact.
- Constraints: Translation panel should not occlude the original source evidence.; Reading direction fixes must precede OCR.; Main message translation should summarize the source, not rewrite unrelated text.; The last sharpening pass must not damage already-corrected text edges.
- Success criteria: Reversal and tilt are corrected before OCR.; Main text becomes readable enough to transcribe.; Translated summary panel is cleanly placed beside the source.; Final image remains legible in both original and translated areas.
- Failure traps: Over-sharpening before OCR and making characters noisy.; Placing the translation over the evidence region.
- Hard negative actions: Applying the final ImageSR before Overlayer.; Using OCR on the raw image.
- Reward focus: orientation dependency, text recovery, translation block layout, late-stage preservation
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Undoing the weather effect around repaired text regions.; Sharpening restored surfaces until they look pasted on.

- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is not just text replacement; it is a recover-extract-edit-reinsert cycle where each step preserves geometry for the next.
- Constraints: Reinsertion should preserve edge continuity around the patch.; Recovery steps should not permanently flatten the final surface.; Extraction must correspond to the true text-bearing patch, not a surrounding decorative area.; Replacement wording must inherit the original curvature and local texture logic.
- Success criteria: Original wording becomes readable enough to guide replacement.; Correct local patch is extracted and edited.; Final text follows curved geometry naturally.; Patch boundaries are visually integrated after reinsertion.
- Failure traps: Extracting too large a patch and damaging surrounding graphics.; Returning a flat-looking text block to a curved surface.
- Hard negative actions: Treating the rotated recovery view as the final output image.; Using ImageEdit before text recovery and patch isolation.
- Reward focus: minimal correction, text recovery, patch extraction, surface reintegration
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This is a long dependency chain where geometry, readability, translation relayout, and late enhancement all interact.
- Constraints: Reading direction fixes must precede OCR.; Translation panel should not occlude the original source evidence.; The last sharpening pass must not damage already-corrected text edges.; Main message translation should summarize the source, not rewrite unrelated text.
- Success criteria: Reversal and tilt are corrected before OCR.; Main text becomes readable enough to transcribe.; Translated summary panel is cleanly placed beside the source.; Final image remains legible in both original and translated areas.
- Failure traps: Placing the translation over the evidence region.; OCR on mirrored input before flipping.

### rl_batch3_0560 | extreme | weather_text_restore_dual_surface_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Make the bus stop ad scene with printed poster and bench label feel like fresh snowfall, but protect two separate readable surfaces. Recover their wording first, edit the scene globally, then locally repair or reapply damaged text areas and only then enhance final clarity. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Do not infer hidden content that is not supported by the image.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This couples one global edit with two separate local text-preservation obligations, forcing the agent to track multiple fragile regions through the chain.
- Constraints: Global weather change should not be reverted when doing local text repair.; Text restoration must remain confined to damaged surface regions.; Final sharpening should not create halos around repaired text edges.; Both text-bearing surfaces must be considered, not just the most prominent one.
- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Sharpening restored surfaces until they look pasted on.; Undoing the weather effect around repaired text regions.
- Hard negative actions: Running ImageSR before local text restoration is finished.; Repairing text globally instead of surface-locally.
- Reward focus: global-local coordination, multi-surface preservation, attachment realism, late enhancement discipline
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- Reward focus: orientation dependency, text recovery, translation block layout, late-stage preservation
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0575 | extreme | weather_text_restore_dual_surface_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: For the bookstore sidewalk scene with sign and poster, change the atmosphere to golden hour after rain while keeping both the main sign and the secondary printed surface readable; OCR them first, perform the global weather edit, then locally restore whichever text-bearing surfaces need repair before a final clarity pass. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Later polish steps must not overwrite earlier localized fixes.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This couples one global edit with two separate local text-preservation obligations, forcing the agent to track multiple fragile regions through the chain.
- Constraints: Both text-bearing surfaces must be considered, not just the most prominent one.; Text restoration must remain confined to damaged surface regions.; Global weather change should not be reverted when doing local text repair.; Final sharpening should not create halos around repaired text edges.
- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Undoing the weather effect around repaired text regions.; Sharpening restored surfaces until they look pasted on.
- Hard negative actions: Running ImageSR before local text restoration is finished.; Repairing text globally instead of surface-locally.
- Reward focus: global-local coordination, multi-surface preservation, attachment realism, late enhancement discipline
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. - Min tool count: 9

- Success criteria: Original wording becomes readable enough to guide replacement.; Correct local patch is extracted and edited.; Final text follows curved geometry naturally.; Patch boundaries are visually integrated after reinsertion.
- Failure traps: Returning a flat-looking text block to a curved surface.; Editing text directly on the raw warped region without recovery.

Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: ImageRotate, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageRotate -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is not just text replacement; it is a recover-extract-edit-reinsert cycle where each step preserves geometry for the next.
- Constraints: Replacement wording must inherit the original curvature and local texture logic.; Recovery steps should not permanently flatten the final surface.; Reinsertion should preserve edge continuity around the patch.; Extraction must correspond to the true text-bearing patch, not a surrounding decorative area.
- Success criteria: Original wording becomes readable enough to guide replacement.; Correct local patch is extracted and edited.; Final text follows curved geometry naturally.; Patch boundaries are visually integrated after reinsertion.
- Failure traps: Editing text directly on the raw warped region without recovery.; Extracting too large a patch and damaging surrounding graphics.
- Hard negative actions: Treating the rotated recovery view as the final output image.; Skipping extraction and trying to overlay flat text back onto the surface.
- Reward focus: minimal correction, text recovery, patch extraction, surface reintegration
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- Why hard: This is a long dependency chain where geometry, readability, translation relayout, and late enhancement all interact.
- Constraints: Main message translation should summarize the source, not rewrite unrelated text.; Translation panel should not occlude the original source evidence.; The last sharpening pass must not damage already-corrected text edges.; Reading direction fixes must precede OCR.
- Success criteria: Reversal and tilt are corrected before OCR.; Main text becomes readable enough to transcribe.; Translated summary panel is cleanly placed beside the source.; Final image remains legible in both original and translated areas.
- Failure traps: Over-sharpening before OCR and making characters noisy.; OCR on mirrored input before flipping.

### rl_batch3_0593 | extreme | weather_text_restore_dual_surface_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Make the bus stop ad scene with printed poster and bench label feel like light rain at dusk, but protect two separate readable surfaces. Recover their wording first, edit the scene globally, then locally repair or reapply damaged text areas and only then enhance final clarity. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. If a step depends on orientation or localization, resolve that dependency first.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This couples one global edit with two separate local text-preservation obligations, forcing the agent to track multiple fragile regions through the chain.
- Constraints: Text restoration must remain confined to damaged surface regions.; Both text-bearing surfaces must be considered, not just the most prominent one.; Global weather change should not be reverted when doing local text repair.; Final sharpening should not create halos around repaired text edges.
- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Saving only the main sign while forgetting the secondary printed surface.; Undoing the weather effect around repaired text regions.
- Hard negative actions: Repairing text globally instead of surface-locally.; Applying the weather edit before recovering what the text says.
- Reward focus: global-local coordination, multi-surface preservation, attachment realism, late enhancement discipline
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- Success criteria: Reversal and tilt are corrected before OCR.; Main text becomes readable enough to transcribe.; Translated summary panel is cleanly placed beside the source.; Final image remains legible in both original and translated areas.
- Failure traps: Over-sharpening before OCR and making characters noisy.; OCR on mirrored input before flipping.
- Hard negative actions: Applying the final ImageSR before Overlayer.; Using OCR on the raw image.
- Reward focus: orientation dependency, text recovery, translation block layout, late-stage preservation
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0608 | extreme | weather_text_restore_dual_surface_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Make the bookstore sidewalk scene with sign and poster feel like overcast drizzle, but protect two separate readable surfaces. Recover their wording first, edit the scene globally, then locally repair or reapply damaged text areas and only then enhance final clarity. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Only terminate when each sub-goal has visual evidence of being satisfied.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This couples one global edit with two separate local text-preservation obligations, forcing the agent to track multiple fragile regions through the chain.
- Constraints: Text restoration must remain confined to damaged surface regions.; Global weather change should not be reverted when doing local text repair.; Both text-bearing surfaces must be considered, not just the most prominent one.; Final sharpening should not create halos around repaired text edges.
- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Saving only the main sign while forgetting the secondary printed surface.; Sharpening restored surfaces until they look pasted on.
- Hard negative actions: Applying the weather edit before recovering what the text says.; Repairing text globally instead of surface-locally.
- Reward focus: global-local coordination, multi-surface preservation, attachment realism, late enhancement discipline
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0609 | extreme | recover_replace_curve_reinsert_verify_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: For the helmet sticker photo with curved lettering, recover distorted text through minimal geometric correction, crop and enhance the local text area, read it, extract the actual surface patch, replace the wording with 'LIMITED BLEND', and place the edited patch back without flattening the surface. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Do not infer hidden content that is not supported by the image.
- Required tools: ImageRotate, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageRotate -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is not just text replacement; it is a recover-extract-edit-reinsert cycle where each step preserves geometry for the next.
- Constraints: Extraction must correspond to the true text-bearing patch, not a surrounding decorative area.; Replacement wording must inherit the original curvature and local texture logic.; Recovery steps should not permanently flatten the final surface.; Reinsertion should preserve edge continuity around the patch.
- Success criteria: Original wording becomes readable enough to guide replacement.; Correct local patch is extracted and edited.; Final text follows curved geometry naturally.; Patch boundaries are visually integrated after reinsertion.
- Failure traps: Extracting too large a patch and damaging surrounding graphics.; Returning a flat-looking text block to a curved surface.
- Hard negative actions: Skipping extraction and trying to overlay flat text back onto the surface.; Using ImageEdit before text recovery and patch isolation.
- Reward focus: minimal correction, text recovery, patch extraction, surface reintegration
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- User prompt: For the arched banner on a sports stand, recover distorted text through minimal geometric correction, crop and enhance the local text area, read it, extract the actual surface patch, replace the wording with 'CITY PASS', and place the edited patch back without flattening the surface. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Do not infer hidden content that is not supported by the image.
- Required tools: ImageRotate, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageRotate -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is not just text replacement; it is a recover-extract-edit-reinsert cycle where each step preserves geometry for the next.
- Constraints: Replacement wording must inherit the original curvature and local texture logic.; Reinsertion should preserve edge continuity around the patch.; Extraction must correspond to the true text-bearing patch, not a surrounding decorative area.; Recovery steps should not permanently flatten the final surface.
- Success criteria: Original wording becomes readable enough to guide replacement.; Correct local patch is extracted and edited.; Final text follows curved geometry naturally.; Patch boundaries are visually integrated after reinsertion.
- Failure traps: Extracting too large a patch and damaging surrounding graphics.; Editing text directly on the raw warped region without recovery.
- Hard negative actions: Treating the rotated recovery view as the final output image.; Using ImageEdit before text recovery and patch isolation.
- Reward focus: minimal correction, text recovery, patch extraction, surface reintegration
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0625 | extreme | document_reverse_recover_translate_relayout_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: For the tilted transit notice reflected in a window, undo reversal and tilt first, crop to the useful text, improve clarity, OCR the content, translate the main message into Arabic, add that translation as a side summary panel, and keep sharpening as the final step only. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Do not infer hidden content that is not supported by the image.
- Required tools: ImageFlip, ImageRotate, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: ImageFlip -> ImageRotate -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This is a long dependency chain where geometry, readability, translation relayout, and late enhancement all interact.
- Constraints: Main message translation should summarize the source, not rewrite unrelated text.; Reading direction fixes must precede OCR.; Translation panel should not occlude the original source evidence.; The last sharpening pass must not damage already-corrected text edges.
- Success criteria: Reversal and tilt are corrected before OCR.; Main text becomes readable enough to transcribe.; Translated summary panel is cleanly placed beside the source.; Final image remains legible in both original and translated areas.
- Failure traps: OCR on mirrored input before flipping.; Placing the translation over the evidence region.
- Hard negative actions: Applying the final ImageSR before Overlayer.; Editing the whole image globally instead of isolating a translation panel.
- Reward focus: orientation dependency, text recovery, translation block layout, late-stage preservation
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0626 | extreme | weather_text_restore_dual_surface_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Make the bus stop ad scene with printed poster and bench label feel like golden hour after rain, but protect two separate readable surfaces. Recover their wording first, edit the scene globally, then locally repair or reapply damaged text areas and only then enhance final clarity. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Do not infer hidden content that is not supported by the image.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This couples one global edit with two separate local text-preservation obligations, forcing the agent to track multiple fragile regions through the chain.
- Constraints: Final sharpening should not create halos around repaired text edges.; Text restoration must remain confined to damaged surface regions.; Both text-bearing surfaces must be considered, not just the most prominent one.; Global weather change should not be reverted when doing local text repair.
- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Saving only the main sign while forgetting the secondary printed surface.; Sharpening restored surfaces until they look pasted on.
- Hard negative actions: Repairing text globally instead of surface-locally.; Running ImageSR before local text restoration is finished.
- Reward focus: global-local coordination, multi-surface preservation, attachment realism, late enhancement discipline
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0627 | extreme | recover_replace_curve_reinsert_verify_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: In the helmet sticker photo with curved lettering, correct the reading angle only as much as necessary, isolate the text-bearing region, recover the wording, extract that local surface, replace the wording with 'LIMITED BLEND', and reinsert it so the curvature still looks physically attached. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. If a step depends on orientation or localization, resolve that dependency first.
- Required tools: ImageRotate, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageRotate -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is not just text replacement; it is a recover-extract-edit-reinsert cycle where each step preserves geometry for the next.
- Constraints: Reinsertion should preserve edge continuity around the patch.; Extraction must correspond to the true text-bearing patch, not a surrounding decorative area.; Recovery steps should not permanently flatten the final surface.; Replacement wording must inherit the original curvature and local texture logic.
- Success criteria: Original wording becomes readable enough to guide replacement.; Correct local patch is extracted and edited.; Final text follows curved geometry naturally.; Patch boundaries are visually integrated after reinsertion.
- Failure traps: Returning a flat-looking text block to a curved surface.; Editing text directly on the raw warped region without recovery.
- Hard negative actions: Skipping extraction and trying to overlay flat text back onto the surface.; Using ImageEdit before text recovery and patch isolation.
- Reward focus: minimal correction, text recovery, patch extraction, surface reintegration
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- Preferred tool order: ImageFlip -> ImageRotate -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This is a long dependency chain where geometry, readability, translation relayout, and late enhancement all interact.
- Constraints: The last sharpening pass must not damage already-corrected text edges.; Translation panel should not occlude the original source evidence.; Reading direction fixes must precede OCR.; Main message translation should summarize the source, not rewrite unrelated text.
- Success criteria: Reversal and tilt are corrected before OCR.; Main text becomes readable enough to transcribe.; Translated summary panel is cleanly placed beside the source.; Final image remains legible in both original and translated areas.
- Failure traps: Placing the translation over the evidence region.; Over-sharpening before OCR and making characters noisy.
- Hard negative actions: Editing the whole image globally instead of isolating a translation panel.; Using OCR on the raw image.
- Reward focus: orientation dependency, text recovery, translation block layout, late-stage preservation
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.

- Constraints: Text restoration must remain confined to damaged surface regions.; Global weather change should not be reverted when doing local text repair.; Both text-bearing surfaces must be considered, not just the most prominent one.; Final sharpening should not create halos around repaired text edges.
- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.

- Reward focus: global-local coordination, multi-surface preservation, attachment realism, late enhancement discipline
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- Min tool count: 8

- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Sharpening restored surfaces until they look pasted on.; Undoing the weather effect around repaired text regions.
