Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Do not infer hidden content that is not supported by the image.
- Required tools: ImageFlip, ImageRotate, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: ImageFlip -> ImageRotate -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This is a long dependency chain where geometry, readability, translation relayout, and late enhancement all interact.
- Constraints: Reading direction fixes must precede OCR.; Translation panel should not occlude the original source evidence.; Main message translation should summarize the source, not rewrite unrelated text.; The last sharpening pass must not damage already-corrected text edges.
- Success criteria: Reversal and tilt are corrected before OCR.; Main text becomes readable enough to transcribe.; Translated summary panel is cleanly placed beside the source.; Final image remains legible in both original and translated areas.
- Failure traps: Placing the translation over the evidence region.; Over-sharpening before OCR and making characters noisy.
- Hard negative actions: Editing the whole image globally instead of isolating a translation panel.; Applying the final ImageSR before Overlayer.
- Reward focus: orientation dependency, text recovery, translation block layout, late-stage preservation
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0674 | extreme | weather_text_restore_dual_surface_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Turn the bookstore sidewalk scene with sign and poster into fresh snowfall, but preserve two different text-bearing surfaces: first recover what those surfaces say, then apply the weather edit, repair or reinsert any damaged text areas locally, and sharpen only after both surfaces are stable. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Do not infer hidden content that is not supported by the image.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This couples one global edit with two separate local text-preservation obligations, forcing the agent to track multiple fragile regions through the chain.
- Constraints: Both text-bearing surfaces must be considered, not just the most prominent one.; Text restoration must remain confined to damaged surface regions.; Final sharpening should not create halos around repaired text edges.; Global weather change should not be reverted when doing local text repair.
- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Saving only the main sign while forgetting the secondary printed surface.; Sharpening restored surfaces until they look pasted on.
- Hard negative actions: Repairing text globally instead of surface-locally.; Running ImageSR before local text restoration is finished.
- Reward focus: global-local coordination, multi-surface preservation, attachment realism, late enhancement discipline
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0675 | extreme | recover_replace_curve_reinsert_verify_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Use the paper bowl rim graphic with bent wording to run a full recover-and-reinsert cycle: make the text readable, extract the correct curved patch, rewrite it to 'LIMITED BLEND', and restore it to the same surface with believable material continuity. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: ImageRotate, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageRotate -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is not just text replacement; it is a recover-extract-edit-reinsert cycle where each step preserves geometry for the next.
- Constraints: Recovery steps should not permanently flatten the final surface.; Replacement wording must inherit the original curvature and local texture logic.; Reinsertion should preserve edge continuity around the patch.; Extraction must correspond to the true text-bearing patch, not a surrounding decorative area.
- Success criteria: Original wording becomes readable enough to guide replacement.; Correct local patch is extracted and edited.; Final text follows curved geometry naturally.; Patch boundaries are visually integrated after reinsertion.
- Failure traps: Extracting too large a patch and damaging surrounding graphics.; Editing text directly on the raw warped region without recovery.
- Hard negative actions: Skipping extraction and trying to overlay flat text back onto the surface.; Using ImageEdit before text recovery and patch isolation.
- Reward focus: minimal correction, text recovery, patch extraction, surface reintegration
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- Constraints: Global weather change should not be reverted when doing local text repair.; Final sharpening should not create halos around repaired text edges.; Both text-bearing surfaces must be considered, not just the most prominent one.; Text restoration must remain confined to damaged surface regions.
- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Undoing the weather effect around repaired text regions.; Saving only the main sign while forgetting the secondary printed surface.
- Hard negative actions: Repairing text globally instead of surface-locally.; Applying the weather edit before recovering what the text says.
- Reward focus: global-local coordination, multi-surface preservation, attachment realism, late enhancement discipline
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- Cross image: False
- User prompt: In the helmet sticker photo with curved lettering, correct the reading angle only as much as necessary, isolate the text-bearing region, recover the wording, extract that local surface, replace the wording with 'ROUTE 24', and reinsert it so the curvature still looks physically attached. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.

- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is not just text replacement; it is a recover-extract-edit-reinsert cycle where each step preserves geometry for the next.
- Constraints: Extraction must correspond to the true text-bearing patch, not a surrounding decorative area.; Reinsertion should preserve edge continuity around the patch.; Replacement wording must inherit the original curvature and local texture logic.; Recovery steps should not permanently flatten the final surface.
- Success criteria: Original wording becomes readable enough to guide replacement.; Correct local patch is extracted and edited.; Final text follows curved geometry naturally.; Patch boundaries are visually integrated after reinsertion.
- Failure traps: Extracting too large a patch and damaging surrounding graphics.; Editing text directly on the raw warped region without recovery.

### rl_batch3_0706 | extreme | document_reverse_recover_translate_relayout_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: For the crooked bilingual instruction card in a selfie shot, undo reversal and tilt first, crop to the useful text, improve clarity, OCR the content, translate the main message into Arabic, add that translation as a side summary panel, and keep sharpening as the final step only. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: ImageFlip, ImageRotate, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: ImageFlip -> ImageRotate -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This is a long dependency chain where geometry, readability, translation relayout, and late enhancement all interact.
- Constraints: Reading direction fixes must precede OCR.; Main message translation should summarize the source, not rewrite unrelated text.; Translation panel should not occlude the original source evidence.; The last sharpening pass must not damage already-corrected text edges.
- Success criteria: Reversal and tilt are corrected before OCR.; Main text becomes readable enough to transcribe.; Translated summary panel is cleanly placed beside the source.; Final image remains legible in both original and translated areas.
- Failure traps: Over-sharpening before OCR and making characters noisy.; Placing the translation over the evidence region.
- Hard negative actions: Using OCR on the raw image.; Editing the whole image globally instead of isolating a translation panel.

- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0707 | extreme | weather_text_restore_dual_surface_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: For the bookstore sidewalk scene with sign and poster, change the atmosphere to foggy morning while keeping both the main sign and the secondary printed surface readable; OCR them first, perform the global weather edit, then locally restore whichever text-bearing surfaces need repair before a final clarity pass. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This couples one global edit with two separate local text-preservation obligations, forcing the agent to track multiple fragile regions through the chain.
- Constraints: Both text-bearing surfaces must be considered, not just the most prominent one.; Final sharpening should not create halos around repaired text edges.; Global weather change should not be reverted when doing local text repair.; Text restoration must remain confined to damaged surface regions.
- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Sharpening restored surfaces until they look pasted on.; Saving only the main sign while forgetting the secondary printed surface.
- Hard negative actions: Applying the weather edit before recovering what the text says.; Repairing text globally instead of surface-locally.
- Reward focus: global-local coordination, multi-surface preservation, attachment realism, late enhancement discipline
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0708 | extreme | recover_replace_curve_reinsert_verify_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: In the curved cosmetic bottle label, correct the reading angle only as much as necessary, isolate the text-bearing region, recover the wording, extract that local surface, replace the wording with 'LIMITED BLEND', and reinsert it so the curvature still looks physically attached. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: ImageRotate, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageRotate -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is not just text replacement; it is a recover-extract-edit-reinsert cycle where each step preserves geometry for the next.
- Constraints: Reinsertion should preserve edge continuity around the patch.; Replacement wording must inherit the original curvature and local texture logic.; Recovery steps should not permanently flatten the final surface.; Extraction must correspond to the true text-bearing patch, not a surrounding decorative area.
- Success criteria: Original wording becomes readable enough to guide replacement.; Correct local patch is extracted and edited.; Final text follows curved geometry naturally.; Patch boundaries are visually integrated after reinsertion.
- Failure traps: Extracting too large a patch and damaging surrounding graphics.; Editing text directly on the raw warped region without recovery.
- Hard negative actions: Skipping extraction and trying to overlay flat text back onto the surface.; Using ImageEdit before text recovery and patch isolation.
- Reward focus: minimal correction, text recovery, patch extraction, surface reintegration
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This couples one global edit with two separate local text-preservation obligations, forcing the agent to track multiple fragile regions through the chain.
- Constraints: Text restoration must remain confined to damaged surface regions.; Both text-bearing surfaces must be considered, not just the most prominent one.; Final sharpening should not create halos around repaired text edges.; Global weather change should not be reverted when doing local text repair.
- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Saving only the main sign while forgetting the secondary printed surface.; Sharpening restored surfaces until they look pasted on.
- Hard negative actions: Applying the weather edit before recovering what the text says.; Running ImageSR before local text restoration is finished.
- Reward focus: global-local coordination, multi-surface preservation, attachment realism, late enhancement discipline
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0723 | extreme | recover_replace_curve_reinsert_verify_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: In the plastic drink cup sleeve with warped text, correct the reading angle only as much as necessary, isolate the text-bearing region, recover the wording, extract that local surface, replace the wording with 'NIGHT RIDE', and reinsert it so the curvature still looks physically attached. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Only terminate when each sub-goal has visual evidence of being satisfied.
- Required tools: ImageRotate, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageRotate -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is not just text replacement; it is a recover-extract-edit-reinsert cycle where each step preserves geometry for the next.
- Constraints: Recovery steps should not permanently flatten the final surface.; Extraction must correspond to the true text-bearing patch, not a surrounding decorative area.; Reinsertion should preserve edge continuity around the patch.; Replacement wording must inherit the original curvature and local texture logic.
- Success criteria: Original wording becomes readable enough to guide replacement.; Correct local patch is extracted and edited.; Final text follows curved geometry naturally.; Patch boundaries are visually integrated after reinsertion.
- Failure traps: Returning a flat-looking text block to a curved surface.; Editing text directly on the raw warped region without recovery.
- Hard negative actions: Skipping extraction and trying to overlay flat text back onto the surface.; Using ImageEdit before text recovery and patch isolation.
- Reward focus: minimal correction, text recovery, patch extraction, surface reintegration
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- Hard negative actions: Skipping extraction and trying to overlay flat text back onto the surface.; Treating the rotated recovery view as the final output image.
- Reward focus: minimal correction, text recovery, patch extraction, surface reintegration
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0740 | extreme | weather_text_restore_dual_surface_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: For the bus stop ad scene with printed poster and bench label, change the atmosphere to foggy morning while keeping both the main sign and the secondary printed surface readable; OCR them first, perform the global weather edit, then locally restore whichever text-bearing surfaces need repair before a final clarity pass. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Later polish steps must not overwrite earlier localized fixes.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This couples one global edit with two separate local text-preservation obligations, forcing the agent to track multiple fragile regions through the chain.
- Constraints: Final sharpening should not create halos around repaired text edges.; Both text-bearing surfaces must be considered, not just the most prominent one.; Global weather change should not be reverted when doing local text repair.; Text restoration must remain confined to damaged surface regions.
- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Sharpening restored surfaces until they look pasted on.; Undoing the weather effect around repaired text regions.
- Hard negative actions: Applying the weather edit before recovering what the text says.; Running ImageSR before local text restoration is finished.
- Reward focus: global-local coordination, multi-surface preservation, attachment realism, late enhancement discipline
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Later polish steps must not overwrite earlier localized fixes.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This couples one global edit with two separate local text-preservation obligations, forcing the agent to track multiple fragile regions through the chain.
- Constraints: Text restoration must remain confined to damaged surface regions.; Both text-bearing surfaces must be considered, not just the most prominent one.; Final sharpening should not create halos around repaired text edges.; Global weather change should not be reverted when doing local text repair.
- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Saving only the main sign while forgetting the secondary printed surface.; Sharpening restored surfaces until they look pasted on.
- Hard negative actions: Repairing text globally instead of surface-locally.; Applying the weather edit before recovering what the text says.
- Reward focus: global-local coordination, multi-surface preservation, attachment realism, late enhancement discipline
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0756 | extreme | recover_replace_curve_reinsert_verify_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: In the plastic drink cup sleeve with warped text, correct the reading angle only as much as necessary, isolate the text-bearing region, recover the wording, extract that local surface, replace the wording with 'LIMITED BLEND', and reinsert it so the curvature still looks physically attached. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Later polish steps must not overwrite earlier localized fixes.
- Required tools: ImageRotate, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageRotate -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is not just text replacement; it is a recover-extract-edit-reinsert cycle where each step preserves geometry for the next.
- Constraints: Replacement wording must inherit the original curvature and local texture logic.; Reinsertion should preserve edge continuity around the patch.; Extraction must correspond to the true text-bearing patch, not a surrounding decorative area.; Recovery steps should not permanently flatten the final surface.
- Success criteria: Original wording becomes readable enough to guide replacement.; Correct local patch is extracted and edited.; Final text follows curved geometry naturally.; Patch boundaries are visually integrated after reinsertion.
- Failure traps: Editing text directly on the raw warped region without recovery.; Extracting too large a patch and damaging surrounding graphics.
- Hard negative actions: Skipping extraction and trying to overlay flat text back onto the surface.; Treating the rotated recovery view as the final output image.
- Reward focus: minimal correction, text recovery, patch extraction, surface reintegration
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0757 | extreme | document_reverse_recover_translate_relayout_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Recover the tilted transit notice reflected in a window into readable form, extract the main message, translate it into Chinese, relayout the translation in a clean side block next to the source document, and apply only late-stage enhancement. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: ImageFlip, ImageRotate, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: ImageFlip -> ImageRotate -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This is a long dependency chain where geometry, readability, translation relayout, and late enhancement all interact.
- Constraints: Translation panel should not occlude the original source evidence.; Reading direction fixes must precede OCR.; Main message translation should summarize the source, not rewrite unrelated text.; The last sharpening pass must not damage already-corrected text edges.
- Success criteria: Reversal and tilt are corrected before OCR.; Main text becomes readable enough to transcribe.; Translated summary panel is cleanly placed beside the source.; Final image remains legible in both original and translated areas.
- Failure traps: Placing the translation over the evidence region.; Over-sharpening before OCR and making characters noisy.
- Hard negative actions: Using OCR on the raw image.; Editing the whole image globally instead of isolating a translation panel.

- Constraints: Recovery steps should not permanently flatten the final surface.; Replacement wording must inherit the original curvature and local texture logic.; Reinsertion should preserve edge continuity around the patch.; Extraction must correspond to the true text-bearing patch, not a surrounding decorative area.
- Success criteria: Original wording becomes readable enough to guide replacement.; Correct local patch is extracted and edited.; Final text follows curved geometry naturally.; Patch boundaries are visually integrated after reinsertion.
- Failure traps: Extracting too large a patch and damaging surrounding graphics.; Editing text directly on the raw warped region without recovery.
- Hard negative actions: Using ImageEdit before text recovery and patch isolation.; Treating the rotated recovery view as the final output image.
- Reward focus: minimal correction, text recovery, patch extraction, surface reintegration
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0772 | extreme | document_reverse_recover_translate_relayout_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Take the tilted transit notice reflected in a window, correct the mirrored and angled reading direction, isolate the important text region, enhance it, recover the text, translate the main message into Spanish, place the translated version as a clean summary block beside the original, and only then sharpen the final result. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. If a step depends on orientation or localization, resolve that dependency first.
- Required tools: ImageFlip, ImageRotate, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: ImageFlip -> ImageRotate -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This is a long dependency chain where geometry, readability, translation relayout, and late enhancement all interact.
- Constraints: Translation panel should not occlude the original source evidence.; Reading direction fixes must precede OCR.; Main message translation should summarize the source, not rewrite unrelated text.; The last sharpening pass must not damage already-corrected text edges.
- Success criteria: Reversal and tilt are corrected before OCR.; Main text becomes readable enough to transcribe.; Translated summary panel is cleanly placed beside the source.; Final image remains legible in both original and translated areas.
- Failure traps: OCR on mirrored input before flipping.; Placing the translation over the evidence region.
- Hard negative actions: Applying the final ImageSR before Overlayer.; Editing the whole image globally instead of isolating a translation panel.
- Reward focus: orientation dependency, text recovery, translation block layout, late-stage preservation
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0773 | extreme | weather_text_restore_dual_surface_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: For the bus stop ad scene with printed poster and bench label, change the atmosphere to light rain at dusk while keeping both the main sign and the secondary printed surface readable; OCR them first, perform the global weather edit, then locally restore whichever text-bearing surfaces need repair before a final clarity pass. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This couples one global edit with two separate local text-preservation obligations, forcing the agent to track multiple fragile regions through the chain.
- Constraints: Both text-bearing surfaces must be considered, not just the most prominent one.; Text restoration must remain confined to damaged surface regions.; Final sharpening should not create halos around repaired text edges.; Global weather change should not be reverted when doing local text repair.
- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Saving only the main sign while forgetting the secondary printed surface.; Undoing the weather effect around repaired text regions.
- Hard negative actions: Repairing text globally instead of surface-locally.; Applying the weather edit before recovering what the text says.
- Reward focus: global-local coordination, multi-surface preservation, attachment realism, late enhancement discipline
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0788 | extreme | weather_text_restore_dual_surface_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Turn the market stall with hanging sign and package labels into golden hour after rain, but preserve two different text-bearing surfaces: first recover what those surfaces say, then apply the weather edit, repair or reinsert any damaged text areas locally, and sharpen only after both surfaces are stable. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This couples one global edit with two separate local text-preservation obligations, forcing the agent to track multiple fragile regions through the chain.
- Constraints: Text restoration must remain confined to damaged surface regions.; Global weather change should not be reverted when doing local text repair.; Both text-bearing surfaces must be considered, not just the most prominent one.; Final sharpening should not create halos around repaired text edges.
- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Sharpening restored surfaces until they look pasted on.; Undoing the weather effect around repaired text regions.
- Hard negative actions: Running ImageSR before local text restoration is finished.; Repairing text globally instead of surface-locally.

- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0789 | extreme | recover_replace_curve_reinsert_verify_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: For the arched banner on a sports stand, recover distorted text through minimal geometric correction, crop and enhance the local text area, read it, extract the actual surface patch, replace the wording with 'SUMMER SET', and place the edited patch back without flattening the surface. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Do not infer hidden content that is not supported by the image.
- Required tools: ImageRotate, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageRotate -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is not just text replacement; it is a recover-extract-edit-reinsert cycle where each step preserves geometry for the next.
- Constraints: Replacement wording must inherit the original curvature and local texture logic.; Extraction must correspond to the true text-bearing patch, not a surrounding decorative area.; Recovery steps should not permanently flatten the final surface.; Reinsertion should preserve edge continuity around the patch.
- Success criteria: Original wording becomes readable enough to guide replacement.; Correct local patch is extracted and edited.; Final text follows curved geometry naturally.; Patch boundaries are visually integrated after reinsertion.
- Failure traps: Editing text directly on the raw warped region without recovery.; Returning a flat-looking text block to a curved surface.
- Hard negative actions: Skipping extraction and trying to overlay flat text back onto the surface.; Treating the rotated recovery view as the final output image.
- Reward focus: minimal correction, text recovery, patch extraction, surface reintegration
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

If a step depends on orientation or localization, resolve that dependency first.

- Constraints: Reading direction fixes must precede OCR.; The last sharpening pass must not damage already-corrected text edges.; Main message translation should summarize the source, not rewrite unrelated text.; Translation panel should not occlude the original source evidence.
- Success criteria: Reversal and tilt are corrected before OCR.; Main text becomes readable enough to transcribe.; Translated summary panel is cleanly placed beside the source.; Final image remains legible in both original and translated areas.
- Failure traps: OCR on mirrored input before flipping.; Over-sharpening before OCR and making characters noisy.

- Required tools: ImageRotate, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageRotate -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is not just text replacement; it is a recover-extract-edit-reinsert cycle where each step preserves geometry for the next.
- Constraints: Recovery steps should not permanently flatten the final surface.; Extraction must correspond to the true text-bearing patch, not a surrounding decorative area.; Reinsertion should preserve edge continuity around the patch.; Replacement wording must inherit the original curvature and local texture logic.
- Success criteria: Original wording becomes readable enough to guide replacement.; Correct local patch is extracted and edited.; Final text follows curved geometry naturally.; Patch boundaries are visually integrated after reinsertion.
- Failure traps: Extracting too large a patch and damaging surrounding graphics.; Editing text directly on the raw warped region without recovery.
- Hard negative actions: Using ImageEdit before text recovery and patch isolation.; Skipping extraction and trying to overlay flat text back onto the surface.
- Reward focus: minimal correction, text recovery, patch extraction, surface reintegration
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0806 | extreme | weather_text_restore_dual_surface_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Make the market stall with hanging sign and package labels feel like fresh snowfall, but protect two separate readable surfaces. Recover their wording first, edit the scene globally, then locally repair or reapply damaged text areas and only then enhance final clarity. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This couples one global edit with two separate local text-preservation obligations, forcing the agent to track multiple fragile regions through the chain.
- Constraints: Both text-bearing surfaces must be considered, not just the most prominent one.; Text restoration must remain confined to damaged surface regions.; Final sharpening should not create halos around repaired text edges.; Global weather change should not be reverted when doing local text repair.
- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Sharpening restored surfaces until they look pasted on.; Undoing the weather effect around repaired text regions.
- Hard negative actions: Running ImageSR before local text restoration is finished.; Repairing text globally instead of surface-locally.
- Reward focus: global-local coordination, multi-surface preservation, attachment realism, late enhancement discipline
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- Failure traps: OCR on mirrored input before flipping.; Placing the translation over the evidence region.
- Hard negative actions: Editing the whole image globally instead of isolating a translation panel.; Applying the final ImageSR before Overlayer.
- Reward focus: orientation dependency, text recovery, translation block layout, late-stage preservation
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- Min tool count: 8

- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Saving only the main sign while forgetting the secondary printed surface.; Undoing the weather effect around repaired text regions.

- User prompt: In the helmet sticker photo with curved lettering, correct the reading angle only as much as necessary, isolate the text-bearing region, recover the wording, extract that local surface, replace the wording with 'SUMMER SET', and reinsert it so the curvature still looks physically attached. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Later polish steps must not overwrite earlier localized fixes.
- Required tools: ImageRotate, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageRotate -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is not just text replacement; it is a recover-extract-edit-reinsert cycle where each step preserves geometry for the next.
- Constraints: Reinsertion should preserve edge continuity around the patch.; Extraction must correspond to the true text-bearing patch, not a surrounding decorative area.; Replacement wording must inherit the original curvature and local texture logic.; Recovery steps should not permanently flatten the final surface.
- Success criteria: Original wording becomes readable enough to guide replacement.; Correct local patch is extracted and edited.; Final text follows curved geometry naturally.; Patch boundaries are visually integrated after reinsertion.
- Failure traps: Extracting too large a patch and damaging surrounding graphics.; Returning a flat-looking text block to a curved surface.
- Hard negative actions: Treating the rotated recovery view as the final output image.; Skipping extraction and trying to overlay flat text back onto the surface.
- Reward focus: minimal correction, text recovery, patch extraction, surface reintegration
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0838 | extreme | document_reverse_recover_translate_relayout_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Recover the mirrored event notice photographed from the side into readable form, extract the main message, translate it into Japanese, relayout the translation in a clean side block next to the source document, and apply only late-stage enhancement. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Only terminate when each sub-goal has visual evidence of being satisfied.
- Required tools: ImageFlip, ImageRotate, ImageCrop, ImageSR, OCR, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: ImageFlip -> ImageRotate -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> OCR -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This is a long dependency chain where geometry, readability, translation relayout, and late enhancement all interact.
- Constraints: Translation panel should not occlude the original source evidence.; Reading direction fixes must precede OCR.; The last sharpening pass must not damage already-corrected text edges.; Main message translation should summarize the source, not rewrite unrelated text.
- Success criteria: Reversal and tilt are corrected before OCR.; Main text becomes readable enough to transcribe.; Translated summary panel is cleanly placed beside the source.; Final image remains legible in both original and translated areas.
- Failure traps: OCR on mirrored input before flipping.; Over-sharpening before OCR and making characters noisy.
- Hard negative actions: Editing the whole image globally instead of isolating a translation panel.; Applying the final ImageSR before Overlayer.
- Reward focus: orientation dependency, text recovery, translation block layout, late-stage preservation
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0839 | extreme | weather_text_restore_dual_surface_chain
- Cross image: False
- User prompt: Turn the street cafe terrace with menu board and window sticker into foggy morning, but preserve two different text-bearing surfaces: first recover what those surfaces say, then apply the weather edit, repair or reinsert any damaged text areas locally, and sharpen only after both surfaces are stable. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Only terminate when each sub-goal has visual evidence of being satisfied.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 8
- Long chain steps: 8
- Why hard: This couples one global edit with two separate local text-preservation obligations, forcing the agent to track multiple fragile regions through the chain.
- Constraints: Global weather change should not be reverted when doing local text repair.; Both text-bearing surfaces must be considered, not just the most prominent one.; Final sharpening should not create halos around repaired text edges.; Text restoration must remain confined to damaged surface regions.
- Success criteria: Weather shift is globally visible.; Primary and secondary text surfaces remain readable or are restored locally.; Local repairs stay attached to their original surfaces.; Final image remains coherent across atmosphere and readability goals.
- Failure traps: Saving only the main sign while forgetting the secondary printed surface.; Sharpening restored surfaces until they look pasted on.
- Hard negative actions: Running ImageSR before local text restoration is finished.; Repairing text globally instead of surface-locally.
- Reward focus: global-local coordination, multi-surface preservation, attachment realism, late enhancement discipline
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0851 | ultra | triple_image_signage_transfer_pipeline
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Use the mall sign photo, blank acrylic panel, and transit station poster wall: recover the readable sign text from the source, isolate the sign region cleanly, rebuild that text into Japanese on the clean panel, then place the finished sign into the destination scene while preserving reflection logic and sharpening only at the end. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Do not infer hidden content that is not supported by the image.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is a true three-image chain with recovery, reconstruction, transfer, and late validation constraints that are highly order-sensitive.
- Constraints: The final sharpen pass should happen only after placement is complete.; Final composite must preserve reflection logic in the destination scene.; Text recovery must happen before sign reconstruction.; Sign isolation must be local and should not drag surrounding facade textures into the new panel.
- Success criteria: Readable source sign text is recovered correctly.; Rebuilt translated sign is confined to the clean panel.; Destination composite matches sign perspective and layer order.; Reflections and highlights remain believable in the final scene.
- Failure traps: Compositing the rebuilt sign before local cleanup is finished.; Sharpening too early and amplifying noise before extraction.
- Hard negative actions: Skipping local extraction and trying to repaint the sign directly into the final scene.; Running the final ImageSR before Overlayer.
- Reward focus: multi-image planning, text recovery, local extraction, composite realism, late-stage enhancement
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0852 | ultra | multi_image_subject_cleanup_storyboard_chain
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: From the four noisy event photos of the same athlete plus a clean title card, isolate the same person consistently across the photo set, recover weak details where needed, build a clean hero cutout, place it onto the cover card, extract any readable name text from the original set, and add that text as a clean title on the final card. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Later polish steps must not overwrite earlier localized fixes.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, OCR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> OCR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: The agent must solve multi-image subject consistency, clean extraction, destination composition, and evidence-grounded titling in one chain.
- Constraints: Text overlay should happen after the hero composition is stabilized.; Identity drift across source photos is unacceptable.; If readable name text exists, it must be taken from the image evidence rather than invented.; Subject extraction must preserve hairline and shoulder edges.
- Success criteria: One stable hero subject is produced from the photo set.; Hero cutout is cleanly composited onto the cover card.; Visible subject naming is recovered only from image evidence.; Final cover looks coherent rather than stitched from multiple sources.
- Failure traps: Adding title before the final subject placement is stable.; Mixing incompatible face angles into one unstable subject.
- Hard negative actions: Running OCR before deciding which source image best supports readable text.; Using Overlayer on the cover before extraction quality is acceptable.
- Reward focus: identity consistency, edge quality, evidence-grounded text, cover composition
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> OCR -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This forces the agent to plan across source extraction, intermediate layout building, and final destination transfer without collapsing steps together.
- Constraints: Label update should remain attached to the object surface.; Object extraction must happen before label rewriting.; Destination placement should preserve believable scale and layer order.; Layout composition should stabilize before the destination-scene transfer.
- Success criteria: Correct object is isolated from the source.; Visible label is updated locally on the object surface.; Intermediate layout looks coherent before final transfer.; Destination scene integration remains plausible after final overlay.
- Failure traps: Placing the updated object directly into the destination without building the intermediate layout.; Polishing the layout too early before object and label are stable.
- Hard negative actions: Applying ImageSR before the intermediate layout is complete.; Skipping OCR and guessing the original label text structure.
- Reward focus: three-stage transfer, surface text attachment, intermediate layout quality, final scene realism
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0854 | ultra | triple_image_signage_transfer_pipeline
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Across the cafe facade photo, replacement lightbox plate, and airport corridor ad frame, extract the real sign content from the first image, enhance weak text before reading it, rebuild the sign in Japanese on the second image, then composite that rebuilt sign into the third image without breaking perspective, reflections, or final realism. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Later polish steps must not overwrite earlier localized fixes.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is a true three-image chain with recovery, reconstruction, transfer, and late validation constraints that are highly order-sensitive.
- Constraints: Final composite must preserve reflection logic in the destination scene.; Text recovery must happen before sign reconstruction.; Sign isolation must be local and should not drag surrounding facade textures into the new panel.; The final sharpen pass should happen only after placement is complete.
- Success criteria: Readable source sign text is recovered correctly.; Rebuilt translated sign is confined to the clean panel.; Destination composite matches sign perspective and layer order.; Reflections and highlights remain believable in the final scene.
- Failure traps: Sharpening too early and amplifying noise before extraction.; Translating reflected or mirrored text instead of the real sign.
- Hard negative actions: Applying global ImageEdit to the destination scene before sign reconstruction exists.; Skipping local extraction and trying to repaint the sign directly into the final scene.
- Reward focus: multi-image planning, text recovery, local extraction, composite realism, late-stage enhancement
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0855 | ultra | multi_image_subject_cleanup_storyboard_chain
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: From the four casual speaker photos plus a presentation opener slide, isolate the same person consistently across the photo set, recover weak details where needed, build a clean hero cutout, place it onto the cover card, extract any readable name text from the original set, and add that text as a clean title on the final card. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Only terminate when each sub-goal has visual evidence of being satisfied.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, OCR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> OCR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: The agent must solve multi-image subject consistency, clean extraction, destination composition, and evidence-grounded titling in one chain.
- Constraints: Identity drift across source photos is unacceptable.; Subject extraction must preserve hairline and shoulder edges.; If readable name text exists, it must be taken from the image evidence rather than invented.; Text overlay should happen after the hero composition is stabilized.
- Success criteria: One stable hero subject is produced from the photo set.; Hero cutout is cleanly composited onto the cover card.; Visible subject naming is recovered only from image evidence.; Final cover looks coherent rather than stitched from multiple sources.
- Failure traps: Mixing incompatible face angles into one unstable subject.; Overlaying guessed title text with no visual basis.
- Hard negative actions: Using Overlayer on the cover before extraction quality is acceptable.; Running OCR before deciding which source image best supports readable text.
- Reward focus: identity consistency, edge quality, evidence-grounded text, cover composition
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is a true three-image chain with recovery, reconstruction, transfer, and late validation constraints that are highly order-sensitive.
- Constraints: Sign isolation must be local and should not drag surrounding facade textures into the new panel.; Final composite must preserve reflection logic in the destination scene.; The final sharpen pass should happen only after placement is complete.; Text recovery must happen before sign reconstruction.
- Success criteria: Readable source sign text is recovered correctly.; Rebuilt translated sign is confined to the clean panel.; Destination composite matches sign perspective and layer order.; Reflections and highlights remain believable in the final scene.
- Failure traps: Translating reflected or mirrored text instead of the real sign.; Compositing the rebuilt sign before local cleanup is finished.
- Hard negative actions: Running the final ImageSR before Overlayer.; Skipping local extraction and trying to repaint the sign directly into the final scene.
- Reward focus: multi-image planning, text recovery, local extraction, composite realism, late-stage enhancement
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0870 | ultra | multi_image_subject_cleanup_storyboard_chain
- Cross image: True

- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, OCR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> OCR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: The agent must solve multi-image subject consistency, clean extraction, destination composition, and evidence-grounded titling in one chain.
- Constraints: Subject extraction must preserve hairline and shoulder edges.; Text overlay should happen after the hero composition is stabilized.; If readable name text exists, it must be taken from the image evidence rather than invented.; Identity drift across source photos is unacceptable.
- Success criteria: One stable hero subject is produced from the photo set.; Hero cutout is cleanly composited onto the cover card.; Visible subject naming is recovered only from image evidence.; Final cover looks coherent rather than stitched from multiple sources.
- Failure traps: Overlaying guessed title text with no visual basis.; Mixing incompatible face angles into one unstable subject.
- Hard negative actions: Trying global editing across all photos instead of isolating the subject.; Running OCR before deciding which source image best supports readable text.
- Reward focus: identity consistency, edge quality, evidence-grounded text, cover composition
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0887 | ultra | triple_image_signage_transfer_pipeline
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Across the cafe facade photo, replacement lightbox plate, and airport corridor ad frame, extract the real sign content from the first image, enhance weak text before reading it, rebuild the sign in Arabic on the second image, then composite that rebuilt sign into the third image without breaking perspective, reflections, or final realism. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Later polish steps must not overwrite earlier localized fixes.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is a true three-image chain with recovery, reconstruction, transfer, and late validation constraints that are highly order-sensitive.
- Constraints: Final composite must preserve reflection logic in the destination scene.; Sign isolation must be local and should not drag surrounding facade textures into the new panel.; Text recovery must happen before sign reconstruction.; The final sharpen pass should happen only after placement is complete.
- Success criteria: Readable source sign text is recovered correctly.; Rebuilt translated sign is confined to the clean panel.; Destination composite matches sign perspective and layer order.; Reflections and highlights remain believable in the final scene.
- Failure traps: Translating reflected or mirrored text instead of the real sign.; Sharpening too early and amplifying noise before extraction.
- Hard negative actions: Skipping local extraction and trying to repaint the sign directly into the final scene.; Running the final ImageSR before Overlayer.
- Reward focus: multi-image planning, text recovery, local extraction, composite realism, late-stage enhancement
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, OCR, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> OCR -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This forces the agent to plan across source extraction, intermediate layout building, and final destination transfer without collapsing steps together.
- Constraints: Object extraction must happen before label rewriting.; Label update should remain attached to the object surface.; Destination placement should preserve believable scale and layer order.; Layout composition should stabilize before the destination-scene transfer.
- Success criteria: Correct object is isolated from the source.; Visible label is updated locally on the object surface.; Intermediate layout looks coherent before final transfer.; Destination scene integration remains plausible after final overlay.
- Failure traps: Treating the label as floating text disconnected from the object.; Polishing the layout too early before object and label are stable.
- Hard negative actions: Using Overlayer on the destination scene before the ad layout exists.; Applying ImageSR before the intermediate layout is complete.

- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0902 | ultra | triple_image_signage_transfer_pipeline
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Work across the mall sign photo, blank acrylic panel, and transit station poster wall: localize and recover the physical signage from the source view, translate it into Japanese, reconstruct it on the blank sign panel, and transfer that result into the destination street scene with only a late clarity pass. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is a true three-image chain with recovery, reconstruction, transfer, and late validation constraints that are highly order-sensitive.
- Constraints: Final composite must preserve reflection logic in the destination scene.; Text recovery must happen before sign reconstruction.; The final sharpen pass should happen only after placement is complete.; Sign isolation must be local and should not drag surrounding facade textures into the new panel.
- Success criteria: Readable source sign text is recovered correctly.; Rebuilt translated sign is confined to the clean panel.; Destination composite matches sign perspective and layer order.; Reflections and highlights remain believable in the final scene.
- Failure traps: Sharpening too early and amplifying noise before extraction.; Translating reflected or mirrored text instead of the real sign.
- Hard negative actions: Skipping local extraction and trying to repaint the sign directly into the final scene.; Running the final ImageSR before Overlayer.
- Reward focus: multi-image planning, text recovery, local extraction, composite realism, late-stage enhancement
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0903 | ultra | multi_image_subject_cleanup_storyboard_chain
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Across the four noisy event photos of the same athlete plus a clean title card, stabilize subject identity from the source images, create one clean extracted hero figure, transfer that figure to the title card, then OCR any readable name information from the original photos and overlay it as a neat final heading. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Only terminate when each sub-goal has visual evidence of being satisfied.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, OCR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> OCR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: The agent must solve multi-image subject consistency, clean extraction, destination composition, and evidence-grounded titling in one chain.
- Constraints: If readable name text exists, it must be taken from the image evidence rather than invented.; Text overlay should happen after the hero composition is stabilized.; Subject extraction must preserve hairline and shoulder edges.; Identity drift across source photos is unacceptable.
- Success criteria: One stable hero subject is produced from the photo set.; Hero cutout is cleanly composited onto the cover card.; Visible subject naming is recovered only from image evidence.; Final cover looks coherent rather than stitched from multiple sources.
- Failure traps: Mixing incompatible face angles into one unstable subject.; Overlaying guessed title text with no visual basis.
- Hard negative actions: Trying global editing across all photos instead of isolating the subject.; Using Overlayer on the cover before extraction quality is acceptable.
- Reward focus: identity consistency, edge quality, evidence-grounded text, cover composition
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. If a step depends on orientation or localization, resolve that dependency first.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is a true three-image chain with recovery, reconstruction, transfer, and late validation constraints that are highly order-sensitive.
- Constraints: Sign isolation must be local and should not drag surrounding facade textures into the new panel.; Text recovery must happen before sign reconstruction.; Final composite must preserve reflection logic in the destination scene.; The final sharpen pass should happen only after placement is complete.
- Success criteria: Readable source sign text is recovered correctly.; Rebuilt translated sign is confined to the clean panel.; Destination composite matches sign perspective and layer order.; Reflections and highlights remain believable in the final scene.
- Failure traps: Translating reflected or mirrored text instead of the real sign.; Sharpening too early and amplifying noise before extraction.
- Hard negative actions: Running the final ImageSR before Overlayer.; Applying global ImageEdit to the destination scene before sign reconstruction exists.
- Reward focus: multi-image planning, text recovery, local extraction, composite realism, late-stage enhancement
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0918 | ultra | multi_image_subject_cleanup_storyboard_chain
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Across the four casual speaker photos plus a presentation opener slide, stabilize subject identity from the source images, create one clean extracted hero figure, transfer that figure to the title card, then OCR any readable name information from the original photos and overlay it as a neat final heading. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, OCR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> OCR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: The agent must solve multi-image subject consistency, clean extraction, destination composition, and evidence-grounded titling in one chain.
- Constraints: Subject extraction must preserve hairline and shoulder edges.; If readable name text exists, it must be taken from the image evidence rather than invented.; Text overlay should happen after the hero composition is stabilized.; Identity drift across source photos is unacceptable.
- Success criteria: One stable hero subject is produced from the photo set.; Hero cutout is cleanly composited onto the cover card.; Visible subject naming is recovered only from image evidence.; Final cover looks coherent rather than stitched from multiple sources.
- Failure traps: Overlaying guessed title text with no visual basis.; Mixing incompatible face angles into one unstable subject.
- Hard negative actions: Trying global editing across all photos instead of isolating the subject.; Using Overlayer on the cover before extraction quality is acceptable.
- Reward focus: identity consistency, edge quality, evidence-grounded text, cover composition
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0919 | ultra | cross_image_object_label_update_story_chain
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Across the sports bib photo, clean promo card, and stadium backdrop, extract the text-bearing hero object from the source, recover its label text, rebuild that label to 'NIGHT RACE', place the updated object into the clean layout, sharpen the layout result, then overlay that finished layout into the destination scene. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Do not infer hidden content that is not supported by the image.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, OCR, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> OCR -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This forces the agent to plan across source extraction, intermediate layout building, and final destination transfer without collapsing steps together.
- Constraints: Object extraction must happen before label rewriting.; Layout composition should stabilize before the destination-scene transfer.; Label update should remain attached to the object surface.; Destination placement should preserve believable scale and layer order.
- Success criteria: Correct object is isolated from the source.; Visible label is updated locally on the object surface.; Intermediate layout looks coherent before final transfer.; Destination scene integration remains plausible after final overlay.
- Failure traps: Treating the label as floating text disconnected from the object.; Placing the updated object directly into the destination without building the intermediate layout.
- Hard negative actions: Using Overlayer on the destination scene before the ad layout exists.; Applying ImageSR before the intermediate layout is complete.
- Reward focus: three-stage transfer, surface text attachment, intermediate layout quality, final scene realism
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.
