### rl_batch3_0933 | ultra | multi_image_subject_cleanup_storyboard_chain
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: From the four travel portraits of the same guide plus a blank story cover, isolate the same person consistently across the photo set, recover weak details where needed, build a clean hero cutout, place it onto the cover card, extract any readable name text from the original set, and add that text as a clean title on the final card. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, OCR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> OCR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: The agent must solve multi-image subject consistency, clean extraction, destination composition, and evidence-grounded titling in one chain.
- Constraints: Subject extraction must preserve hairline and shoulder edges.; If readable name text exists, it must be taken from the image evidence rather than invented.; Text overlay should happen after the hero composition is stabilized.; Identity drift across source photos is unacceptable.
- Success criteria: One stable hero subject is produced from the photo set.; Hero cutout is cleanly composited onto the cover card.; Visible subject naming is recovered only from image evidence.; Final cover looks coherent rather than stitched from multiple sources.
- Failure traps: Adding title before the final subject placement is stable.; Overlaying guessed title text with no visual basis.
- Hard negative actions: Using Overlayer on the cover before extraction quality is acceptable.; Running OCR before deciding which source image best supports readable text.
- Reward focus: identity consistency, edge quality, evidence-grounded text, cover composition
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0934 | ultra | cross_image_object_label_update_story_chain
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Use the product bottle photo, blank ad layout, and lifestyle background scene as a three-stage transfer: isolate the main object, read and update its visible label to 'LIMITED DROP', composite the updated object into the ad layout, polish the layout, and finally place the completed layout into the destination environment. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Only terminate when each sub-goal has visual evidence of being satisfied.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, OCR, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> OCR -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This forces the agent to plan across source extraction, intermediate layout building, and final destination transfer without collapsing steps together.
- Constraints: Destination placement should preserve believable scale and layer order.; Object extraction must happen before label rewriting.; Label update should remain attached to the object surface.; Layout composition should stabilize before the destination-scene transfer.
- Success criteria: Correct object is isolated from the source.; Visible label is updated locally on the object surface.; Intermediate layout looks coherent before final transfer.; Destination scene integration remains plausible after final overlay.
- Failure traps: Placing the updated object directly into the destination without building the intermediate layout.; Polishing the layout too early before object and label are stable.
- Hard negative actions: Using Overlayer on the destination scene before the ad layout exists.; Skipping OCR and guessing the original label text structure.
- Reward focus: three-stage transfer, surface text attachment, intermediate layout quality, final scene realism
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0935 | ultra | triple_image_signage_transfer_pipeline
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Use the bookshop window photo, neutral signboard panel, and nighttime avenue scene: recover the readable sign text from the source, isolate the sign region cleanly, rebuild that text into Chinese on the clean panel, then place the finished sign into the destination scene while preserving reflection logic and sharpening only at the end. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is a true three-image chain with recovery, reconstruction, transfer, and late validation constraints that are highly order-sensitive.
- Constraints: Sign isolation must be local and should not drag surrounding facade textures into the new panel.; The final sharpen pass should happen only after placement is complete.; Final composite must preserve reflection logic in the destination scene.; Text recovery must happen before sign reconstruction.
- Success criteria: Readable source sign text is recovered correctly.; Rebuilt translated sign is confined to the clean panel.; Destination composite matches sign perspective and layer order.; Reflections and highlights remain believable in the final scene.
- Failure traps: Compositing the rebuilt sign before local cleanup is finished.; Sharpening too early and amplifying noise before extraction.
- Hard negative actions: Applying global ImageEdit to the destination scene before sign reconstruction exists.; Running the final ImageSR before Overlayer.
- Reward focus: multi-image planning, text recovery, local extraction, composite realism, late-stage enhancement
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0936 | ultra | multi_image_subject_cleanup_storyboard_chain
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Across the four noisy event photos of the same athlete plus a clean title card, stabilize subject identity from the source images, create one clean extracted hero figure, transfer that figure to the title card, then OCR any readable name information from the original photos and overlay it as a neat final heading. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, OCR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> OCR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: The agent must solve multi-image subject consistency, clean extraction, destination composition, and evidence-grounded titling in one chain.
- Constraints: Subject extraction must preserve hairline and shoulder edges.; If readable name text exists, it must be taken from the image evidence rather than invented.; Identity drift across source photos is unacceptable.; Text overlay should happen after the hero composition is stabilized.
- Success criteria: One stable hero subject is produced from the photo set.; Hero cutout is cleanly composited onto the cover card.; Visible subject naming is recovered only from image evidence.; Final cover looks coherent rather than stitched from multiple sources.
- Failure traps: Mixing incompatible face angles into one unstable subject.; Overlaying guessed title text with no visual basis.
- Hard negative actions: Using Overlayer on the cover before extraction quality is acceptable.; Trying global editing across all photos instead of isolating the subject.
- Reward focus: identity consistency, edge quality, evidence-grounded text, cover composition
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0937 | ultra | cross_image_object_label_update_story_chain
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Use the sports bib photo, clean promo card, and stadium backdrop as a three-stage transfer: isolate the main object, read and update its visible label to 'LIMITED DROP', composite the updated object into the ad layout, polish the layout, and finally place the completed layout into the destination environment. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. If a step depends on orientation or localization, resolve that dependency first.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, OCR, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> OCR -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This forces the agent to plan across source extraction, intermediate layout building, and final destination transfer without collapsing steps together.
- Constraints: Destination placement should preserve believable scale and layer order.; Layout composition should stabilize before the destination-scene transfer.; Object extraction must happen before label rewriting.; Label update should remain attached to the object surface.
- Success criteria: Correct object is isolated from the source.; Visible label is updated locally on the object surface.; Intermediate layout looks coherent before final transfer.; Destination scene integration remains plausible after final overlay.
- Failure traps: Treating the label as floating text disconnected from the object.; Polishing the layout too early before object and label are stable.
- Hard negative actions: Applying ImageSR before the intermediate layout is complete.; Skipping OCR and guessing the original label text structure.
- Reward focus: three-stage transfer, surface text attachment, intermediate layout quality, final scene realism
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Only terminate when each sub-goal has visual evidence of being satisfied.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR

- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is a true three-image chain with recovery, reconstruction, transfer, and late validation constraints that are highly order-sensitive.
- Constraints: The final sharpen pass should happen only after placement is complete.; Text recovery must happen before sign reconstruction.; Final composite must preserve reflection logic in the destination scene.; Sign isolation must be local and should not drag surrounding facade textures into the new panel.
- Success criteria: Readable source sign text is recovered correctly.; Rebuilt translated sign is confined to the clean panel.; Destination composite matches sign perspective and layer order.; Reflections and highlights remain believable in the final scene.
- Failure traps: Compositing the rebuilt sign before local cleanup is finished.; Sharpening too early and amplifying noise before extraction.
- Hard negative actions: Skipping local extraction and trying to repaint the sign directly into the final scene.; Running the final ImageSR before Overlayer.

- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0949 | ultra | cross_image_object_label_update_story_chain
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Across the product bottle photo, blank ad layout, and lifestyle background scene, extract the text-bearing hero object from the source, recover its label text, rebuild that label to 'SEASON MENU', place the updated object into the clean layout, sharpen the layout result, then overlay that finished layout into the destination scene. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. If a step depends on orientation or localization, resolve that dependency first.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, OCR, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> OCR -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This forces the agent to plan across source extraction, intermediate layout building, and final destination transfer without collapsing steps together.
- Constraints: Label update should remain attached to the object surface.; Object extraction must happen before label rewriting.; Destination placement should preserve believable scale and layer order.; Layout composition should stabilize before the destination-scene transfer.
- Success criteria: Correct object is isolated from the source.; Visible label is updated locally on the object surface.; Intermediate layout looks coherent before final transfer.; Destination scene integration remains plausible after final overlay.
- Failure traps: Placing the updated object directly into the destination without building the intermediate layout.; Treating the label as floating text disconnected from the object.
- Hard negative actions: Applying ImageSR before the intermediate layout is complete.; Using Overlayer on the destination scene before the ad layout exists.
- Reward focus: three-stage transfer, surface text attachment, intermediate layout quality, final scene realism
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0950 | ultra | triple_image_signage_transfer_pipeline
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Work across the source storefront photo, clean facade plate, and rainy street mockup: localize and recover the physical signage from the source view, translate it into Arabic, reconstruct it on the blank sign panel, and transfer that result into the destination street scene with only a late clarity pass. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. If a step depends on orientation or localization, resolve that dependency first.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is a true three-image chain with recovery, reconstruction, transfer, and late validation constraints that are highly order-sensitive.
- Constraints: Text recovery must happen before sign reconstruction.; Final composite must preserve reflection logic in the destination scene.; The final sharpen pass should happen only after placement is complete.; Sign isolation must be local and should not drag surrounding facade textures into the new panel.
- Success criteria: Readable source sign text is recovered correctly.; Rebuilt translated sign is confined to the clean panel.; Destination composite matches sign perspective and layer order.; Reflections and highlights remain believable in the final scene.
- Failure traps: Compositing the rebuilt sign before local cleanup is finished.; Sharpening too early and amplifying noise before extraction.
- Hard negative actions: Skipping local extraction and trying to repaint the sign directly into the final scene.; Applying global ImageEdit to the destination scene before sign reconstruction exists.
- Reward focus: multi-image planning, text recovery, local extraction, composite realism, late-stage enhancement
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0951 | ultra | multi_image_subject_cleanup_storyboard_chain
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Use the four noisy event photos of the same athlete plus a clean title card to create a consistent cover image: select the best subject geometry across the source photos, normalize detail and identity, extract the final hero subject, composite it onto the blank cover, then recover and overlay the subject name if visible. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. If a step depends on orientation or localization, resolve that dependency first.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, OCR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> OCR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: The agent must solve multi-image subject consistency, clean extraction, destination composition, and evidence-grounded titling in one chain.
- Constraints: Identity drift across source photos is unacceptable.; Subject extraction must preserve hairline and shoulder edges.; Text overlay should happen after the hero composition is stabilized.; If readable name text exists, it must be taken from the image evidence rather than invented.
- Success criteria: One stable hero subject is produced from the photo set.; Hero cutout is cleanly composited onto the cover card.; Visible subject naming is recovered only from image evidence.; Final cover looks coherent rather than stitched from multiple sources.
- Failure traps: Overlaying guessed title text with no visual basis.; Mixing incompatible face angles into one unstable subject.
- Hard negative actions: Running OCR before deciding which source image best supports readable text.; Trying global editing across all photos instead of isolating the subject.
- Reward focus: identity consistency, edge quality, evidence-grounded text, cover composition
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

- Success criteria: One stable hero subject is produced from the photo set.; Hero cutout is cleanly composited onto the cover card.; Visible subject naming is recovered only from image evidence.; Final cover looks coherent rather than stitched from multiple sources.
- Failure traps: Adding title before the final subject placement is stable.; Overlaying guessed title text with no visual basis.

### rl_batch3_0965 | ultra | triple_image_signage_transfer_pipeline
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Across the bookshop window photo, neutral signboard panel, and nighttime avenue scene, extract the real sign content from the first image, enhance weak text before reading it, rebuild the sign in Spanish on the second image, then composite that rebuilt sign into the third image without breaking perspective, reflections, or final realism. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Do not infer hidden content that is not supported by the image.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is a true three-image chain with recovery, reconstruction, transfer, and late validation constraints that are highly order-sensitive.
- Constraints: Text recovery must happen before sign reconstruction.; The final sharpen pass should happen only after placement is complete.; Final composite must preserve reflection logic in the destination scene.; Sign isolation must be local and should not drag surrounding facade textures into the new panel.
- Success criteria: Readable source sign text is recovered correctly.; Rebuilt translated sign is confined to the clean panel.; Destination composite matches sign perspective and layer order.; Reflections and highlights remain believable in the final scene.
- Failure traps: Compositing the rebuilt sign before local cleanup is finished.; Sharpening too early and amplifying noise before extraction.
- Hard negative actions: Skipping local extraction and trying to repaint the sign directly into the final scene.; Running the final ImageSR before Overlayer.
- Reward focus: multi-image planning, text recovery, local extraction, composite realism, late-stage enhancement
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0966 | ultra | multi_image_subject_cleanup_storyboard_chain
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: From the four noisy event photos of the same athlete plus a clean title card, isolate the same person consistently across the photo set, recover weak details where needed, build a clean hero cutout, place it onto the cover card, extract any readable name text from the original set, and add that text as a clean title on the final card. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, OCR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> OCR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: The agent must solve multi-image subject consistency, clean extraction, destination composition, and evidence-grounded titling in one chain.
- Constraints: Subject extraction must preserve hairline and shoulder edges.; Identity drift across source photos is unacceptable.; If readable name text exists, it must be taken from the image evidence rather than invented.; Text overlay should happen after the hero composition is stabilized.
- Success criteria: One stable hero subject is produced from the photo set.; Hero cutout is cleanly composited onto the cover card.; Visible subject naming is recovered only from image evidence.; Final cover looks coherent rather than stitched from multiple sources.
- Failure traps: Overlaying guessed title text with no visual basis.; Adding title before the final subject placement is stable.
- Hard negative actions: Running OCR before deciding which source image best supports readable text.; Trying global editing across all photos instead of isolating the subject.
- Reward focus: identity consistency, edge quality, evidence-grounded text, cover composition
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0967 | ultra | cross_image_object_label_update_story_chain
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: From the sports bib photo, clean promo card, and stadium backdrop, lift the primary label-bearing object, recover and replace its text with 'LIMITED DROP', assemble it into the blank layout, sharpen only after that layout is stable, and then insert the full layout into the destination scene. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Do not infer hidden content that is not supported by the image.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, OCR, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> OCR -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This forces the agent to plan across source extraction, intermediate layout building, and final destination transfer without collapsing steps together.
- Constraints: Layout composition should stabilize before the destination-scene transfer.; Destination placement should preserve believable scale and layer order.; Object extraction must happen before label rewriting.; Label update should remain attached to the object surface.
- Success criteria: Correct object is isolated from the source.; Visible label is updated locally on the object surface.; Intermediate layout looks coherent before final transfer.; Destination scene integration remains plausible after final overlay.
- Failure traps: Treating the label as floating text disconnected from the object.; Polishing the layout too early before object and label are stable.
- Hard negative actions: Applying ImageSR before the intermediate layout is complete.; Using Overlayer on the destination scene before the ad layout exists.
- Reward focus: three-stage transfer, surface text attachment, intermediate layout quality, final scene realism
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0968 | ultra | triple_image_signage_transfer_pipeline
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Work across the bookshop window photo, neutral signboard panel, and nighttime avenue scene: localize and recover the physical signage from the source view, translate it into Spanish, reconstruct it on the blank sign panel, and transfer that result into the destination street scene with only a late clarity pass. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Later polish steps must not overwrite earlier localized fixes.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is a true three-image chain with recovery, reconstruction, transfer, and late validation constraints that are highly order-sensitive.
- Constraints: Sign isolation must be local and should not drag surrounding facade textures into the new panel.; Text recovery must happen before sign reconstruction.; Final composite must preserve reflection logic in the destination scene.; The final sharpen pass should happen only after placement is complete.
- Success criteria: Readable source sign text is recovered correctly.; Rebuilt translated sign is confined to the clean panel.; Destination composite matches sign perspective and layer order.; Reflections and highlights remain believable in the final scene.
- Failure traps: Translating reflected or mirrored text instead of the real sign.; Compositing the rebuilt sign before local cleanup is finished.
- Hard negative actions: Running the final ImageSR before Overlayer.; Skipping local extraction and trying to repaint the sign directly into the final scene.
- Reward focus: multi-image planning, text recovery, local extraction, composite realism, late-stage enhancement
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0969 | ultra | multi_image_subject_cleanup_storyboard_chain
- Cross image: True

Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Later polish steps must not overwrite earlier localized fixes.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, OCR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> OCR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: The agent must solve multi-image subject consistency, clean extraction, destination composition, and evidence-grounded titling in one chain.
- Constraints: Text overlay should happen after the hero composition is stabilized.; Identity drift across source photos is unacceptable.; If readable name text exists, it must be taken from the image evidence rather than invented.; Subject extraction must preserve hairline and shoulder edges.
- Success criteria: One stable hero subject is produced from the photo set.; Hero cutout is cleanly composited onto the cover card.; Visible subject naming is recovered only from image evidence.; Final cover looks coherent rather than stitched from multiple sources.
- Failure traps: Overlaying guessed title text with no visual basis.; Mixing incompatible face angles into one unstable subject.
- Hard negative actions: Running OCR before deciding which source image best supports readable text.; Using Overlayer on the cover before extraction quality is acceptable.
- Reward focus: identity consistency, edge quality, evidence-grounded text, cover composition
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0981 | ultra | multi_image_subject_cleanup_storyboard_chain
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Across the four travel portraits of the same guide plus a blank story cover, stabilize subject identity from the source images, create one clean extracted hero figure, transfer that figure to the title card, then OCR any readable name information from the original photos and overlay it as a neat final heading. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, OCR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> OCR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: The agent must solve multi-image subject consistency, clean extraction, destination composition, and evidence-grounded titling in one chain.
- Constraints: If readable name text exists, it must be taken from the image evidence rather than invented.; Identity drift across source photos is unacceptable.; Subject extraction must preserve hairline and shoulder edges.; Text overlay should happen after the hero composition is stabilized.
- Success criteria: One stable hero subject is produced from the photo set.; Hero cutout is cleanly composited onto the cover card.; Visible subject naming is recovered only from image evidence.; Final cover looks coherent rather than stitched from multiple sources.
- Failure traps: Adding title before the final subject placement is stable.; Overlaying guessed title text with no visual basis.
- Hard negative actions: Running OCR before deciding which source image best supports readable text.; Using Overlayer on the cover before extraction quality is acceptable.
- Reward focus: identity consistency, edge quality, evidence-grounded text, cover composition
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0982 | ultra | cross_image_object_label_update_story_chain
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Use the sports bib photo, clean promo card, and stadium backdrop as a three-stage transfer: isolate the main object, read and update its visible label to 'LIMITED DROP', composite the updated object into the ad layout, polish the layout, and finally place the completed layout into the destination environment. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. If a step depends on orientation or localization, resolve that dependency first.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, OCR, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> OCR -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This forces the agent to plan across source extraction, intermediate layout building, and final destination transfer without collapsing steps together.
- Constraints: Layout composition should stabilize before the destination-scene transfer.; Label update should remain attached to the object surface.; Destination placement should preserve believable scale and layer order.; Object extraction must happen before label rewriting.
- Success criteria: Correct object is isolated from the source.; Visible label is updated locally on the object surface.; Intermediate layout looks coherent before final transfer.; Destination scene integration remains plausible after final overlay.
- Failure traps: Placing the updated object directly into the destination without building the intermediate layout.; Polishing the layout too early before object and label are stable.
- Hard negative actions: Skipping OCR and guessing the original label text structure.; Using Overlayer on the destination scene before the ad layout exists.
- Reward focus: three-stage transfer, surface text attachment, intermediate layout quality, final scene realism
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0983 | ultra | triple_image_signage_transfer_pipeline
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Use the bookshop window photo, neutral signboard panel, and nighttime avenue scene: recover the readable sign text from the source, isolate the sign region cleanly, rebuild that text into Spanish on the clean panel, then place the finished sign into the destination scene while preserving reflection logic and sharpening only at the end. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Assume the final check will compare edited targets against untouched context.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is a true three-image chain with recovery, reconstruction, transfer, and late validation constraints that are highly order-sensitive.
- Constraints: Text recovery must happen before sign reconstruction.; Final composite must preserve reflection logic in the destination scene.; The final sharpen pass should happen only after placement is complete.; Sign isolation must be local and should not drag surrounding facade textures into the new panel.
- Success criteria: Readable source sign text is recovered correctly.; Rebuilt translated sign is confined to the clean panel.; Destination composite matches sign perspective and layer order.; Reflections and highlights remain believable in the final scene.
- Failure traps: Translating reflected or mirrored text instead of the real sign.; Compositing the rebuilt sign before local cleanup is finished.
- Hard negative actions: Applying global ImageEdit to the destination scene before sign reconstruction exists.; Skipping local extraction and trying to repaint the sign directly into the final scene.
- Reward focus: multi-image planning, text recovery, local extraction, composite realism, late-stage enhancement
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. ### rl_batch3_0997 | ultra | cross_image_object_label_update_story_chain
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: From the sports bib photo, clean promo card, and stadium backdrop, lift the primary label-bearing object, recover and replace its text with 'SEASON MENU', assemble it into the blank layout, sharpen only after that layout is stable, and then insert the full layout into the destination scene. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Do not let enhancement steps rewrite structure that earlier steps established. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Later polish steps must not overwrite earlier localized fixes.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, OCR, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> OCR -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This forces the agent to plan across source extraction, intermediate layout building, and final destination transfer without collapsing steps together.
- Constraints: Object extraction must happen before label rewriting.; Layout composition should stabilize before the destination-scene transfer.; Destination placement should preserve believable scale and layer order.; Label update should remain attached to the object surface.
- Success criteria: Correct object is isolated from the source.; Visible label is updated locally on the object surface.; Intermediate layout looks coherent before final transfer.; Destination scene integration remains plausible after final overlay.
- Failure traps: Treating the label as floating text disconnected from the object.; Placing the updated object directly into the destination without building the intermediate layout.
- Hard negative actions: Using Overlayer on the destination scene before the ad layout exists.; Skipping OCR and guessing the original label text structure.
- Reward focus: three-stage transfer, surface text attachment, intermediate layout quality, final scene realism
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0998 | ultra | triple_image_signage_transfer_pipeline
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Use the mall sign photo, blank acrylic panel, and transit station poster wall: recover the readable sign text from the source, isolate the sign region cleanly, rebuild that text into Chinese on the clean panel, then place the finished sign into the destination scene while preserving reflection logic and sharpening only at the end. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Treat untouched regions as part of the evaluation target, not just the edited area. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. If a step depends on orientation or localization, resolve that dependency first.
- Required tools: OCR, ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR
- Preferred tool order: OCR -> ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This is a true three-image chain with recovery, reconstruction, transfer, and late validation constraints that are highly order-sensitive.
- Constraints: The final sharpen pass should happen only after placement is complete.; Sign isolation must be local and should not drag surrounding facade textures into the new panel.; Text recovery must happen before sign reconstruction.; Final composite must preserve reflection logic in the destination scene.
- Success criteria: Readable source sign text is recovered correctly.; Rebuilt translated sign is confined to the clean panel.; Destination composite matches sign perspective and layer order.; Reflections and highlights remain believable in the final scene.
- Failure traps: Sharpening too early and amplifying noise before extraction.; Translating reflected or mirrored text instead of the real sign.
- Hard negative actions: Applying global ImageEdit to the destination scene before sign reconstruction exists.; Running the final ImageSR before Overlayer.
- Reward focus: multi-image planning, text recovery, local extraction, composite realism, late-stage enhancement
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_0999 | ultra | multi_image_subject_cleanup_storyboard_chain
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: From the four noisy event photos of the same athlete plus a clean title card, isolate the same person consistently across the photo set, recover weak details where needed, build a clean hero cutout, place it onto the cover card, extract any readable name text from the original set, and add that text as a clean title on the final card. Preserve object identity through every extraction and transfer step. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Keep geometry, material cues, and readability jointly consistent. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Later polish steps must not overwrite earlier localized fixes.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageCrop, ImageSR, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, ImageEdit, Overlayer, OCR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageCrop -> ImageSR -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> OCR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: The agent must solve multi-image subject consistency, clean extraction, destination composition, and evidence-grounded titling in one chain.
- Constraints: Subject extraction must preserve hairline and shoulder edges.; Text overlay should happen after the hero composition is stabilized.; Identity drift across source photos is unacceptable.; If readable name text exists, it must be taken from the image evidence rather than invented.
- Success criteria: One stable hero subject is produced from the photo set.; Hero cutout is cleanly composited onto the cover card.; Visible subject naming is recovered only from image evidence.; Final cover looks coherent rather than stitched from multiple sources.
- Failure traps: Mixing incompatible face angles into one unstable subject.; Overlaying guessed title text with no visual basis.
- Hard negative actions: Running OCR before deciding which source image best supports readable text.; Trying global editing across all photos instead of isolating the subject.
- Reward focus: identity consistency, edge quality, evidence-grounded text, cover composition
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.

### rl_batch3_1000 | ultra | cross_image_object_label_update_story_chain
- Cross image: True
- User prompt: Use the street sign source shot, poster mockup, and destination travel scene as a three-stage transfer: isolate the main object, read and update its visible label to 'SEASON MENU', composite the updated object into the ad layout, polish the layout, and finally place the completed layout into the destination environment. Keep scale, perspective, and layer order believable at every stage. Do not use a shortcut that sacrifices local correctness for global smoothness. Assume a reviewer will zoom into both edges and text-bearing surfaces. Use conservative local corrections when multiple interpretations are possible. Only terminate when each sub-goal has visual evidence of being satisfied.
- Required tools: ImageGrounding, ImageSAM, ImageExtract, OCR, ImageCrop, ImageEdit, Overlayer, ImageSR, Overlayer
- Preferred tool order: ImageGrounding -> ImageSAM -> ImageExtract -> OCR -> ImageCrop -> ImageEdit -> Overlayer -> ImageSR -> Overlayer
- Min tool count: 9
- Long chain steps: 9
- Why hard: This forces the agent to plan across source extraction, intermediate layout building, and final destination transfer without collapsing steps together.
- Constraints: Layout composition should stabilize before the destination-scene transfer.; Destination placement should preserve believable scale and layer order.; Object extraction must happen before label rewriting.; Label update should remain attached to the object surface.
- Success criteria: Correct object is isolated from the source.; Visible label is updated locally on the object surface.; Intermediate layout looks coherent before final transfer.; Destination scene integration remains plausible after final overlay.
- Failure traps: Polishing the layout too early before object and label are stable.; Placing the updated object directly into the destination without building the intermediate layout.
- Hard negative actions: Skipping OCR and guessing the original label text structure.; Applying ImageSR before the intermediate layout is complete.
- Reward focus: three-stage transfer, surface text attachment, intermediate layout quality, final scene realism
- Termination: Stop only when all sub-goals are satisfied, later polishing has not broken earlier local fixes, and untouched context still looks stable.
